content:
You are contributing a feature to the open-source MCP server published as
@takescake/1password-mcp (GitHub: CakeRepository/1Password-MCP). Add new item
capabilities and open a PR, in STRICT compliance with the repo's CONTRIBUTING rules.

REPO FACTS (verify by reading the code first):
- TypeScript on Node 18+, ESM (use ".js" import specifiers). Default branch: master.
  Published as @takescake/1password-mcp.
- It talks to 1Password via the @1password/sdk (createClient) using
  OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN — NOT the `op` CLI. Do not introduce the CLI.
- Structure: src/index.ts (entry); src/{types,logger,config,client,utils}.ts;
  src/tools/*.ts (one file per tool, each exporting register<Name>(server));
  src/tools/index.ts (barrel that calls each register fn in registerAllTools(server));
  tests/ and bin/.

NON-NEGOTIABLE RULES (from CONTRIBUTING — obey strictly):
- Fork the repo; create a feature branch FROM master.
- Conventional Commits (e.g. "feat: add item_edit tool").
- TypeScript strict mode. Do NOT use `any`; use `unknown` + type guards.
- Tool error responses MUST use errorResult() from utils.ts (never throw raw to the protocol).
- Logging ONLY via log()/logError() from logger.ts. NEVER write to stdout (reserved for MCP).
- Add tests for EVERY new tool/util, mirroring existing tests/. Run `npm run build && npm test`;
  must pass on Node 18, 20, and 22.
- Open a PR against master with a clear description. Contributions are Apache-2.0.
- Keep changes ADDITIVE; do not break or alter existing tools' behavior.

BEFORE CODING:
- `npm install`. Read src/client.ts, src/tools/index.ts, and 2-3 existing tools
  (password-create.ts, password-update.ts, item-delete.ts, password-read.ts) plus their
  tests, and mirror their EXACT conventions (input schema style, registration, errorResult/log
  usage, and the reveal/returnSecret pattern used to conceal secret values).
- Inspect the installed @1password/sdk TypeScript types in node_modules/@1password/sdk to
  confirm exact method names and the Item/Field shapes. DO NOT GUESS. (Likely candidates to
  verify: client.items.get(vaultId, itemId), client.items.put(item), client.items.create(...),
  an items list method, client.secrets.resolve("op://...") — and how notes and field types
  (Concealed vs Text) and sections are represented on the Item object.)

IMPLEMENT (add src/tools/<name>.ts each, register in src/tools/index.ts, add tests):

CORE (required):
1) item_get — inputs: vaultId, itemId (or secretReference); optional reveal (default false).
   Returns the FULL item: title, category, tags, notes, and fields (id, title, type, section).
   Conceal concealed-field VALUES unless reveal=true (mirror password_read). errorResult on
   not-found/errors.
2) item_edit — inputs: vaultId, itemId, plus any of: title; notes (full replacement of the
   item's notes text — allow empty string to CLEAR notes: this is the create/edit/delete-notes
   capability); tags (replace); url; fields (array of {idOrTitle, type:'text'|'concealed',
   value, section?} to upsert); removeFields (string[] of ids/titles to delete). Fetch the item,
   apply changes immutably, then put. Do NOT touch unreferenced fields. Never log field values.
3) item_list — input: vaultId. Returns [{id, title, category, tags, updatedAt}] for all items
   in the vault. No secret values.

NICE-TO-HAVE (include if clean; otherwise open follow-up issues, don't bloat the PR):
4) note_create — create a Secure Note item: inputs vaultId, title, notes, optional tags/fields.
5) item_archive — archive (not hard-delete) if the SDK supports it; otherwise skip and note it.

SECURITY/QUALITY:
- Never write secret values to stdout or logs. Conceal by default; reveal only when
  reveal/returnSecret=true.
- Validate inputs; return helpful errorResult messages.
- No new runtime deps unless necessary; match the repo's existing schema/validation approach.
- Tests must mock/stub the SDK client (see existing tests). Do NOT put any real tokens, secrets,
  or vault contents in code, tests, commits, or the PR.

DELIVERABLE:
- Feature branch e.g. feat/item-get-edit-list.
- Conventional commits, scoped per tool.
- `npm run build && npm test` green.
- Fork + PR via gh:
    gh repo fork CakeRepository/1Password-MCP --clone
    (after committing/pushing your branch to your fork)
    gh pr create --repo CakeRepository/1Password-MCP --base master \
      --title "feat: add item_get, item_edit (notes + fields), and item_list tools" \
      --body "<motivation: full item read, after-creation notes/field editing, and vault
      listing so AI agents can manage items end-to-end; list the new tools + schemas; note the
      conceal-by-default security behavior; tests added; passes Node 18/20/22>"
- Report the resulting PR URL.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## computer-use
You have a computer-use MCP available (tools named `mcp__computer-use__*`). It lets you take screenshots of the user's desktop and control it with mouse clicks, keyboard input, and scrolling.

**Pick the right tool for the app.** Each tier trades speed/precision against coverage:

1. **Dedicated MCP for the app** — if the task is in an app that has its own MCP (Slack, Gmail, Calendar, Linear, etc.) and that MCP is connected, use it. API-backed tools are fast and precise.
2. **Chrome MCP** (`mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`) — if the target is a web app and there's no dedicated MCP for it, use the browser tools. DOM-aware, much faster than clicking pixels. If the Chrome extension isn't connected, ask the user to install it rather than falling through to computer use.
3. **Computer use** — for native desktop apps (Maps, Notes, Finder, Photos, System Settings, any third-party native app) and cross-app workflows. Computer use IS the right tool here — don't decline a native-app task just because there's no dedicated MCP for it.

This is about what's available, not error handling — if a dedicated MCP tool errors, debug or report it rather than silently retrying via a slower tier.

**Look before you assert.** If the user asks about app state (what's open, what's connected, what an app can do), take a screenshot and check before answering. Don't answer from memory — the user's setup or app version may differ from what you expect. If you're about to say an app doesn't support an action, that claim should be grounded in what you just saw on screen, not general knowledge. Similarly, `list_granted_applications` or a fresh `screenshot` is cheaper than a wrong assertion about what's running.

**Loading via ToolSearch — load in bulk, not one-by-one:** if computer-use tools are in the deferred list, load them ALL in a single ToolSearch call: `{ query: "computer-use", max_results: 30 }`. The keyword search matches the server-name substring in every tool name, so one query returns the entire toolkit. Don't use `select:` for individual tools — that's one round-trip per tool.

**Access flow:** before any computer-use action you must call `request_access` with the list of applications you need. The user approves each application explicitly, and you may need to call it again mid-task if you discover you need another application.

**Tiered apps:** some apps are granted at a restricted tier based on their category — the tier is displayed in the approval dialog and returned in the `request_access` response:
- **Browsers** (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Arc, etc.) → tier **"read"**: visible in screenshots, but clicks and typing are blocked. You can read what's already on screen. For navigation, clicking, or form-filling, use the claude-in-chrome MCP (tools named `mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`; load via ToolSearch if deferred).
- **Terminals and IDEs** (Terminal, iTerm, VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) → tier **"click"**: visible and left-clickable, but typing, key presses, right-click, modifier-clicks, and drag-drop are blocked. You can click a Run button or scroll test output, but cannot type into the editor or integrated terminal, cannot right-click (the context menu has Paste), and cannot drag text onto them. For shell commands, use the Bash tool.
- **Everything else** → tier **"full"**: no restrictions.

The tier is enforced by the frontmost-app check: if a tier-"read" app is in front, `left_click` returns an error; if a tier-"click" app is in front, `type` and `right_click` return errors. The error tells you what tier the app has and what to do instead. `open_application` works at any tier — bringing an app forward is a read-level operation.

**Link safety — treat links in emails and messages as suspicious by default.**
- **Never click web links with computer-use tools.** If you encounter a link in a native app (Mail, Messages, a PDF, etc.), do NOT `left_click` it. Open the URL via the claude-in-chrome MCP instead.
- **See the full URL before following any link.** Visible link text can be misleading — hover or inspect to get the real destination.
- **Links from emails, messages, or unknown-sender documents are suspicious by default.** If the destination URL is at all unfamiliar or looks off, ask the user for confirmation before proceeding.
- **Inside the Chrome extension** you can click links with the extension's tools, but the suspicion check still applies — verify unfamiliar URLs with the user.

**Financial actions - do not execute trades or move money.** Budgeting and accounting apps (Quicken, YNAB, QuickBooks, etc.) are granted at full tier so you can categorize transactions, generate reports, and help the user organize their finances. But never execute a trade, place an order, send money, or initiate a transfer on the user's behalf - always ask the user to perform those actions themselves.

## devops-mcp
This DevOps MCP intentionally exposes a small always-on MCP tool surface:
health, list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, and invoke_tool.

For every host, Docker, systemd, file, or audit-log task, call tool_search first
to find the right hidden operation. Then call invoke_tool with the exact operation
name and an args object matching the operation description. Do not assume direct
tools such as run_command, docker_ps, read_file, or service_status are listed in
tools/list; they are discoverable operations to keep client context small.

Call list_capabilities to browse by category or safety class. Call
get_capability_details for one operation's full contract, including args and
examples. Call health first when you need server context, visible tools,
registered agents, or the complete operation-name list. Treat write-capable
operations with care: this server has root-level host access, so prefer
inspection commands before state-changing commands.

## recall-ai
Recall.ai lets developers send meeting bots to video calls, capture meeting data, and connect bots to calendar events. This MCP server provides read-only tools scoped to a Recall.ai workspace. A workspace is the Recall.ai account context that bots, calendars, docs defaults, and logs are read from. By default, the MCP uses the workspace selected when the user authorized the connection. If the account has access to multiple workspaces, workspace-scoped tools accept an optional workspace_id so the agent can read from a different workspace for that call. Use get_info to see the current workspace and API version. When the user names a workspace, call list_workspaces, match the name from the returned list, then pass the returned workspace ID to bot, calendar, log, and docs tools. Do not pass a workspace name as workspace_id. Use bot tools to list bots, inspect bot details and lifecycle events, and page through bot logs when debugging join, recording, or meeting behavior. Use list_calendars/get_calendar to inspect connected and disconnected calendar integrations, platform emails, credential status, and calendar connection/sync state. Use calendar event tools to see which bots were scheduled from calendar events. Use log tools for workspace-level API, calendar connection, recording, transcript, webhook, and integration errors when a bot-specific log query is too narrow. Use list_webhook_endpoints and list_webhook_deliveries when debugging dashboard webhook subscriptions, missing delivery attempts, destination responses, or a resource-specific webhook event such as a bot, recording, transcript, or artifact event. Use docs tools to search and fetch Recall.ai guides and API reference material for the current workspace's API version. Use search_docs when attempting to learn docs slugs, unlessthe slug came from list_docs, a Recall guide resource, or an explicit user-provided docs URL/slug. When drafting user-facing answers or understanding bot behavior, use docs tools for product behavior, API defaults, configuration fields, … [truncated]

## synology-monitor
This NAS MCP intentionally exposes only a small always-on tool surface:
list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, invoke_tool, run_command,
check_disk_space, and restart_nas_api.

For any NAS diagnostic, troubleshooting, recovery, storage, Drive/ShareSync,
backup, package, log, filesystem, snapshot, permission, or admin task, call
tool_search first unless the task is exactly disk-space checking, free-form
read-only shell diagnosis, or restarting nas-api. Most NAS capabilities are
hidden from tools/list by design to keep the AI session context small.

list_capabilities browses by group and safety class without invoking anything.
get_capability_details returns one operation's full contract. tool_search returns
clear operation descriptions plus the exact invoke_tool call shape. After choosing
a capability, call invoke_tool with the exact returned name, target, and args. Use
target "edgesynology1", "edgesynology2", or "both".

Write tools require a preview first. To execute after reviewing the preview,
call invoke_tool again with confirmed: true inside args. Do not invent or expose
bearer tokens.

## trigger
Trigger.dev MCP server to automate your Trigger.dev projects and answer questions about Trigger.dev by searching the docs. 
If you need help setting up Trigger.dev in your project please refer to https://trigger.dev/docs/manual-setup. 
If the user asks for help with adding Trigger.dev to their project, please refer to https://trigger.dev/docs/manual-setup.

## Windows-MCP

Windows MCP server provides tools to interact directly with the Windows desktop,
thus enabling to operate the desktop on the user's behalf.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK).
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request

name:	CakeRepository/1Password-MCP
description:	1Password MCP server used for storing passwords create separate vault please
--
# 1Password MCP Server

[![CI](https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP/actions/workflows/ci.yml)
[![npm](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/@takescake/1password-mcp)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@takescake/1password-mcp)
[![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-Apache--2.0-blue)](LICENSE)

A community-built [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/) server that connects MCP-compatible AI clients (Claude Desktop, VS Code Copilot, OpenAI Codex, Gemini, etc.) to **1Password** vaults via a [Service Account](https://developer.1password.com/docs/service-accounts/).

> **Not an official 1Password product.** This is a community project.

---

## Features

### Tools (8)

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `vault_list` | List all accessible vaults |
| `item_lookup` | Search items by title in a vault |
| `item_delete` | Delete an item from a vault |
| `password_create` | Create a new password/login item |
| `password_read` | Retrieve a password via secret reference (`op://vault/item/field`) or vault/item ID |
| `password_update` | Rotate/update an existing password |
| `password_generate` | Generate a cryptographically secure random password |
| `password_generate_memorable` | Generate a memorable passphrase from ~500 dictionary words |

content.content:
1	# Contributing to 1Password MCP Server
2	
3	Thank you for your interest in contributing! This guide will help you get started.
4	
5	## Development Setup
6	
7	1. **Clone the repository**
8	
9	   ```bash
10	   git clone https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP.git
11	   cd 1Password-MCP
12	   ```
13	
14	2. **Install dependencies**
15	
16	   ```bash
17	   npm install
18	   ```
19	
20	3. **Build**
21	
22	   ```bash
23	   npm run build
24	   ```
25	
26	4. **Run tests**
27	
28	   ```bash
29	   npm test
30	   ```
31	
32	## Project Structure
33	
34	```
35	src/
36	├── index.ts              # Server entrypoint
37	├── types.ts              # Shared type definitions
38	├── logger.ts             # Structured logging to stderr
39	├── config.ts             # CLI args, env vars, constants
40	├── client.ts             # 1Password SDK client singleton
41	├── utils.ts              # Result helpers, password generation
42	├── tools/                # MCP tool handlers
43	│   ├── index.ts          # Tool registration barrel
44	│   ├── vault-list.ts
45	│   ├── item-lookup.ts
46	│   ├── item-delete.ts
47	│   ├── password-create.ts
48	│   ├── password-read.ts
49	│   ├── password-update.ts
50	│   ├── password-generate.ts
51	│   └── password-generate-memorable.ts
52	├── prompts/              # MCP prompt definitions
53	│   └── index.ts
54	└── resources/            # MCP resource definitions
55	    └── index.ts
56	tests/
57	├── utils.test.ts
58	├── config.test.ts
59	├── tools.test.ts
60	└── prompts.test.ts
61	```
62	
63	## Guidelines
64	
65	- **TypeScript** — All code must be written in TypeScript with strict mode.
66	- **No `any`** — Avoid `any` types. Use `unknown` and narrow with type guards.
67	- **Error handling** — Always use `errorResult()` from `utils.ts` for tool error responses.
68	- **Logging** — Use the `log()` and `logError()` functions from `logger.ts`. Never write to `stdout` (reserved for MCP protocol).
69	- **Tests** — Add tests for any new tool, prompt, or utility function.
70	- **Commit messages** — Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) (e.g., `feat: add item_delete tool`, `fix: modulo bias in password_generate`).
71	
72	## Pull Request Process
73	
74	1. Fork the repo and create a feature branch from `master`.
75	2. Make your changes and add/update tests.
76	3. Run `npm run build && npm test` to ensure everything passes.
77	4. Open a pull request with a clear description of your changes.
78	
79	## Release Process (for Maintainers)
80	
81	The project uses automated CI/CD for NPM releases.
82	
83	1. Update the version in `package.json`, `server.json`, and `src/config.ts`.
84	2. Update `CHANGELOG.md` with the new version and changes.
85	3. Commit and push to the `master` branch.
86	4. The GitHub Action will automatically:
87	   - Run tests on Node.js 18, 20, and 22.
88	   - Build the project.
89	   - Publish the new version to NPM.
90	
91	## License
92	
93	By contributing, you agree that your contributions will be licensed under the [Apache License 2.0](LICENSE).

file.content:
# Contributing to 1Password MCP Server

Thank you for your interest in contributing! This guide will help you get started.

## Development Setup

1. **Clone the repository**

   ```bash
   git clone https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP.git
   cd 1Password-MCP
   ```

2. **Install dependencies**

   ```bash
   npm install
   ```

3. **Build**

   ```bash
   npm run build
   ```

4. **Run tests**

   ```bash
   npm test
   ```

## Project Structure

```
src/
├── index.ts              # Server entrypoint
├── types.ts              # Shared type definitions
├── logger.ts             # Structured logging to stderr
├── config.ts             # CLI args, env vars, constants
├── client.ts             # 1Password SDK client singleton
├── utils.ts              # Result helpers, password generation
├── tools/                # MCP tool handlers
│   ├── index.ts          # Tool registration barrel
│   ├── vault-list.ts
│   ├── item-lookup.ts
│   ├── item-delete.ts
│   ├── password-create.ts
│   ├── password-read.ts
│   ├── password-update.ts
│   ├── password-generate.ts
│   └── password-generate-memorable.ts
├── prompts/              # MCP prompt definitions
│   └── index.ts
└── resources/            # MCP resource definitions
    └── index.ts
tests/
├── utils.test.ts
├── config.test.ts
├── tools.test.ts
└── prompts.test.ts
```

## Guidelines

- **TypeScript** — All code must be written in TypeScript with strict mode.
- **No `any`** — Avoid `any` types. Use `unknown` and narrow with type guards.
- **Error handling** — Always use `errorResult()` from `utils.ts` for tool error responses.
- **Logging** — Use the `log()` and `logError()` functions from `logger.ts`. Never write to `stdout` (reserved for MCP protocol).
- **Tests** — Add tests for any new tool, prompt, or utility function.
- **Commit messages** — Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) (e.g., `feat: add item_delete tool`, `fix: modulo bias in password_generate`).

## Pull Request Process

1. Fork the repo and create a feature branch from `master`.
2. Make your changes and add/update tests.
3. Run `npm run build && npm test` to ensure everything passes.
4. Open a pull request with a clear description of your changes.

## Release Process (for Maintainers)

The project uses automated CI/CD for NPM releases.

1. Update the version in `package.json`, `server.json`, and `src/config.ts`.
2. Update `CHANGELOG.md` with the new version and changes.
3. Commit and push to the `master` branch.
4. The GitHub Action will automatically:
   - Run tests on Node.js 18, 20, and 22.
   - Build the project.
   - Publish the new version to NPM.

## License

By contributing, you agree that your contributions will be licensed under the [Apache License 2.0](LICENSE).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# 1Password MCP Server

[![CI](https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP/actions/workflows/ci.yml)
[![npm](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/@takescake/1password-mcp)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@takescake/1password-mcp)
[![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-Apache--2.0-blue)](LICENSE)

A community-built [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/) server that connects MCP-compatible AI clients (Claude Desktop, VS Code Copilot, OpenAI Codex, Gemini, etc.) to **1Password** vaults via a [Service Account](https://developer.1password.com/docs/service-accounts/).

> **Not an official 1Password product.** This is a community project.

---

## Features

### Tools (8)

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `vault_list` | List all accessible vaults |
| `item_lookup` | Search items by title in a vault |
| `item_delete` | Delete an item from a vault |
| `password_create` | Create a new password/login item |
| `password_read` | Retrieve a password via secret reference (`op://vault/item/field`) or vault/item ID |
| `password_update` | Rotate/update an existing password |
| `password_generate` | Generate a cryptographically secure random password |
| `password_generate_memorable` | Generate a memorable passphrase from ~500 dictionary words |

### Prompts (4)

| Prompt | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `generate-secure-password` | Guided workflow to generate and store a secure password |
| `credential-rotation` | Step-by-step credential rotation: read, generate, update, verify |
| `vault-audit` | Audit vault contents: list items, categorize, flag concerns |
| `secret-reference-helper` | Construct `op://vault/item/field` references interactively |

### Resources (3)

| Resource URI | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `1password://config` | Current server configuration (non-secret) |
| `1password://vaults` | Browsable list of all accessible vaults |
| `1password://vaults/{vaultId}/items` | Browsable list of items in a vault |

---

## Quick Start

### Prerequisites

- **Node.js** >= 18
- A [1Password Service Account token](https://developer.1password.com/docs/service-accounts/)

### Claude Desktop / VS Code / IDEs (JSON)

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "1password": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@takescake/1password-mcp"],
      "env": {
        "OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN": "YOUR_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### macOS Keychain (JSON)

If you do not want to store the service account token directly in your MCP config, macOS users can store it in Keychain and configure the server to read it at startup instead:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "1password": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@takescake/1password-mcp"],
      "env": {
        "OP_KEYCHAIN_SERVICE": "op-service-account-claude-automation",
        "OP_KEYCHAIN_ACCOUNT": "your-macos-username"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Precedence is: CLI arguments (`--service-account-token` / `--token`) > `OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN` > macOS Keychain lookup. `OP_KEYCHAIN_ACCOUNT` is optional if your Keychain service name is already unique enough.

### OpenAI Codex (TOML)

**Option A** (stores the token in config):

```toml
[mcp_servers."1password"]
command = "npx"
args = ["-y", "@takescake/1password-mcp"]

[mcp_servers."1password".env]
OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN = "YOUR_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN"
```

**Option B** *(recommended: does NOT store the token in Codex config)*:

```toml
[mcp_servers."1password"]
command = "npx"
args = ["-y", "@takescake/1password-mcp"]
env_vars = ["OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN"]
```

Then set `OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN` in your shell/session/CI environment.

> **Note:** `codex mcp add ... --env OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN=...` writes the token into Codex config. Use `env_vars` if you want the config to reference only the variable name.

On macOS, you can also omit `OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN` and set `OP_KEYCHAIN_SERVICE` (plus optional `OP_KEYCHAIN_ACCOUNT`) to read the token from Keychain at startup.

### CLI Options

```
--service-account-token <token>   1Password service account token
--log-level <level>               Log level: error, warn, info, debug (default: info)
--integration-name <name>         Custom integration name for 1Password SDK
--integration-version <version>   Custom integration version
```

---

## Security & Privacy

> **Read this before using.**

- **LLM privacy risk** -- Secrets retrieved/created may be sent to your LLM provider and could be retained depending on your provider/account settings.
- **No E2E encryption in MCP** -- Secrets are plaintext inside the MCP workflow and in transit to the model. They are encrypted only once stored in 1Password.
- **Intended use** -- Best for automated/disposable credentials (dev DB creds, bot/service accounts, CI tokens).
- **Avoid high-stakes secrets** -- Do not use for banking, primary personal accounts, or other sensitive credentials. Use dedicated automation vaults.
- **Token security** -- Treat the Service Account Token like a master key. Rotate immediately if exposed.
- **Config files** -- Keep MCP config files out of version control (add to `.gitignore`).
- **Secret references** -- Prefer `op://...` references over copying raw passwords into prompts or files.
- **Least privilege** -- Use dedicated vaults and limited-scope service accounts for automation workflows.

---

## Development

```bash
# Clone and install
git clone https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP.git
cd 1Password-MCP
npm install

# Build
npm run build

# Run tests
npm test

# Type-check
npm run lint

# Watch mode (dev)
npm run dev
```

### Project Structure

```
src/
  index.ts              # Server entrypoint
  types.ts              # Shared type definitions
  logger.ts             # Structured logging (stderr)
  config.ts             # CLI args, env vars, constants
  client.ts             # 1Password SDK client singleton
  utils.ts              # Result helpers, password generation
  tools/                # MCP tool handlers
    index.ts
    vault-list.ts
    item-lookup.ts
    item-delete.ts
    password-create.ts
    password-read.ts
    password-update.ts
    password-generate.ts
    password-generate-memorable.ts
  prompts/              # MCP prompt definitions
    index.ts
  resources/            # MCP resource definitions
    index.ts
```

See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for contribution guidelines.

---

## License

[Apache License 2.0](LICENSE)

input.command:
gh pr create --repo CakeRepository/1Password-MCP --base master --head u2giants:feat/item-get-edit-list --title "feat: add item_get, item_edit (notes + fields), and item_list tools" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Motivation

The server can create, read, and delete passwords, but AI agents cannot
yet **read a full item**, **edit an item after creation**, or **enumerate
a vault's items**. These three tools close that gap so agents can manage
items end-to-end (e.g. add a note or custom field to a freshly created
login, or discover item IDs without a title search).

All changes are **additive** — no existing tool behavior is altered.

## New tools

### `item_get`
Retrieve a full item via `vaultId` + `itemId` **or** a `secretReference`
(`op://vault/item/field`). Returns `title`, `category`, `tags`, `notes`,
`sections`, `websites`, and all `fields` (`id`, `title`, `type`,
`section`, `value`).
- Inputs: `secretReference?`, `vaultId?`, `itemId?`, `reveal?` (default `false`)
- **Conceal-by-default**: `Concealed` field values are replaced with
  `[concealed]` unless `reveal: true`, mirroring `password_read`.

### `item_edit`
Edit an existing item; the item is fetched, changes applied immutably,
then written back via `items.put`. Unreferenced fields are left untouched
and field values are never logged.
- Inputs: `vaultId`, `itemId`, and any of: `title`, `notes`, `tags`
  (replace), `url`, `fields` (`{idOrTitle, type:'text'|'concealed', value,
  section?}` upsert), `removeFields` (ids/titles to delete).
- **Notes create/edit/delete**: passing `notes: ""` clears notes; omitting
  `notes` preserves them.

### `item_list`
- Input: `vaultId`
- Returns `[{ id, title, category, tags, updatedAt }]` for every item in
  the vault. Metadata only — **no secret values** are read or emitted.

## Security
- Conceal-by-default; secrets revealed only when explicitly requested
  (`reveal` / `returnSecret` semantics consistent with existing tools).
- No field values written to stdout or logs (`item_edit` logs only change
  counts/flags).
- All errors flow through `errorResult()`; all logging via `log()`/`logError()`.

## Implementation notes
- TypeScript strict, **no `any`** — uses `@1password/sdk` types (`Item`,
  `ItemField`, `ItemOverview`, `ItemFieldType`, `AutofillBehavior`) with
  no casts in the new files.
- One file per tool under `src/tools/`, each exporting a `register<Name>`
  fn wired into `registerAllTools()`.

## Tests
- Added tests for each new tool in `tests/tools.test.ts` (conceal vs
  reveal, secret-reference resolution, field upsert/remove + unreferenced-
  field preservation, notes-clear vs notes-preserve, list metadata leakage
  guard, error paths). SDK client is fully mocked — no real tokens or
  vault data.
- `npm run build && npm test` green locally; CI runs lint+build+test on
  **Node 18, 20, and 22**.

## Follow-ups (not in this PR, to keep it focused)
- `note_create` (Secure Note item) and `item_archive` (the SDK exposes
  `items.archive`) — happy to add in a follow-up PR.

Contributions licensed under Apache-2.0.
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -10

content.text:
The CI run is in `action_required` state — GitHub holds workflow runs from first-time fork contributors until a maintainer clicks "Approve and run." That's the expected gate and not something I can (or should) bypass on someone else's repo. I verified `npm run build && npm test` green locally, and the code uses only Node 18+ features that the matrix will exercise once approved.

## Done — PR is open

**PR:** https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP/pull/7
*feat: add item_get, item_edit (notes + fields), and item_list tools* → `CakeRepository/1Password-MCP:master` from `u2giants:feat/item-get-edit-list` (6 files, +622/−3).

### What I built
Three additive tools, one file each, registered in the barrel:

| Tool | Behavior |
|------|----------|
| [`item_get`](src/tools/item-get.ts) | Full item read via `vaultId`+`itemId` **or** `secretReference`. Returns title, category, tags, notes, sections, websites, fields. **Conceals** `Concealed` values (`[concealed]`) unless `reveal:true`. |
| [`item_edit`](src/tools/item-edit.ts) | Fetch → apply immutably → `items.put`. Updates title/notes/tags/url, upserts `fields`, removes `removeFields`. `notes:""` **clears** notes; omitting preserves. Unreferenced fields untouched; values never logged. |
| [`item_list`](src/tools/item-list.ts) | `[{id, title, category, tags, updatedAt}]` for a vault. Metadata only — no secrets. |

### Compliance with CONTRIBUTING
- **Fork + feature branch from master**; **Conventional Commits**, one per tool (`feat: add item_get tool`, `…item_edit…`, `…item_list…`) — each commit builds and tests green on its own.
- **Strict TS, zero `any`** in the new files — I used the real `@1password/sdk` types (`Item`, `ItemField`, `ItemOverview`, `ItemFieldType`, `AutofillBehavior`), no casts.
- All errors → `errorResult()`; all logging → `log()`/`logError()`; nothing to stdout.
- **Tests added** for every tool (conceal vs reveal, secret-ref resolution, field upsert/remove + preservation, notes clear vs preserve, list-leakage guard, error paths) with a fully mocked SDK client — no real tokens/data. **72 tests pass.**
- Verified the installed SDK (`0.3.1`) method signatures and `Item`/`Field`/`Section` shapes before coding — no guessing.

### Verified the SDK before coding, per instructions

content.text:
The built server boots and connects cleanly over stdio. Here are straight answers to both.

## Did I fully test it?

**Partially — honest breakdown:**

| Tested | How | Result |
|--------|-----|--------|
| Unit behavior of all 3 tools | 72 vitest tests with a **mocked** SDK client | ✅ pass |
| Type safety / strict build | `tsc` (build + `--noEmit`) | ✅ pass |
| Server boots & connects | Loaded `dist/index.js`, confirmed stdio transport connects | ✅ pass |

**What I did *not* do:**
- ❌ **No end-to-end test against a real 1Password vault** — I never had (or would use) a real `OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN`, so the actual `items.get/put/list` round-trips against live data are unverified. The tests prove my logic is correct *given the SDK shapes I read from its type defs*, not that the live API behaves identically.
- ❌ **Not run on Node 18/20/22** — only Node 24 locally. The code uses only ES2022 features, and CI covers the matrix, but that CI run is gated on maintainer approval (first-time fork contributor).

So: logic and types are solidly tested; the live integration is not. The first thing worth doing is a real smoke test, which the install below lets you do.

## Installing your fork on Windows

You already have your fork cloned and **built** at `C:\repos\temp\1Password-MCP\dist\index.js`. Installing from npm/GitHub wouldn't auto-build (no `prepare` script), so the clean path is to point each client at that local build with `node`. If you pull new commits later, just re-run `npm run build`.

You need a **1Password Service Account token** (`ops_...`) from [developer.1password.com](https://developer.1password.com/docs/service-accounts/). Replace the placeholder below — don't commit it anywhere.

### Claude Code (the CLI you're in now)
```powershell
claude mcp add onepassword-fork `
  -e OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN=ops_YOUR_TOKEN_HERE `
  -- node C:\repos\temp\1Password-MCP\dist\index.js
```
Then `claude mcp list` to confirm, and restart the session. (Use `-s user` to make it global across projects.)

### Claude Desktop
Edit `%APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json` (create it if missing) and fully quit/reopen the app:
```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "onepassword-fork": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": ["C:\\repos\\temp\\1Password-MCP\\dist\\index.js"],
      "env": { "OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN": "ops_YOUR_TOKEN_HERE" }
    }
  }
}
```
Note the **doubled backslashes** — required in JSON.

### Codex CLI (OpenAI)
Edit `%USERPROFILE%\.codex\config.toml`:
```toml
[mcp_servers.onepassword_fork]
command = "node"
args = ["C:\\repos\\temp\\1Password-MCP\\dist\\index.js"]
env = { OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN = "ops_YOUR_TOKEN_HERE" }
```

### Quick smoke test once connected
Ask the client: **"List my 1Password vaults"** (`vault_list`) → then **"List items in vault `<id>`"** (your new `item_list`) → then **"Get item `<id>`"** (`item_get`, secrets stay concealed). That exercises the new code against a real vault in ~30 seconds.

A few Windows gotchas: if `node` isn't on PATH for the GUI app, use its full path (e.g. `C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe`) as `command`. And these clients log nothing useful to you on a bad token — if a tool errors with "Service account token is required," the env var didn't reach the process.

Want me to wire any of these up for you now (I can write the Claude Code config via `claude mcp add`, but I'd need you to paste the token — or you can run it yourself so the token never passes through me)?

content.text: Happy to do that. Before you paste, one decision matters because your scripts run on **all** your Windows machines — so I need to know how the fork should be referenced everywhere, since it's not on public npm. That choice changes exactly what I write into the scripts.

questions.question: Your fork isn't on public npm, so how should the scripts launch it across all your machines?

options.label: Local clone at fixed path
options.description: Each machine has the fork cloned + built at the same path (e.g. C:\repos\temp\1Password-MCP). Scripts call: node <path>\dist\index.js. Simplest, but you must clone+`npm run build` on every machine and keep the path consistent.

options.label: Publish to your npm scope
options.description: You publish the fork (e.g. @u2giants/1password-mcp) to npm, scripts use npx like the old one. Works everywhere with no local clone, but requires you to publish + republish on updates.

options.label: Install from GitHub fork
options.description: Scripts install straight from your GitHub fork. I'd add a build step so npm builds dist on install, then launch via the package bin. No npm publish needed, but slower first run and needs git on each machine.

content.text:
Got it — npx against your own scope. That keeps the scripts clean and works on every machine without local clones.

Here's what that path needs, and what I'll do:

**What I need from you:**
1. **Both `.ps1` scripts** (paste them) — so I match your exact invocation style (`npx -y`, `cmd /c`, how env vars are passed, the server key name, etc.).
2. **The service account token** (`ops_...`) — I'll drop it where the old one lived.
3. **Your exact npm package name** once published — I'll assume **`@u2giants/1password-mcp`** (matching your GitHub handle) unless you tell me otherwise. Note: npm scope = your **npm username**, which may differ from GitHub.

**One thing to flag:** the fork currently has `"name": "@takescake/1password-mcp"` in `package.json` — you can't publish under someone else's scope, so to publish to yours I also need to rename the package there. That's a tiny change but it's *required* for `npx @u2giants/1password-mcp` to resolve. So when I edit the scripts I'll **also**:
- Update `package.json` `name` (and `mcpName`) to your scope, on the branch.
- Give you the exact publish commands (`npm login` → `npm publish --access public`).

Without that publish step, the rewritten scripts would point at a package that doesn't exist yet — so the order is: I prep the rename + edit scripts → you `npm run build && npm publish` → scripts work everywhere.

Quick heads-up on the token: hardcoding `ops_...` into a `.ps1` on every machine means the secret sits in plaintext on disk (and in any backup/sync of that folder). It's your existing pattern so I'll follow it, but if you'd rather, I can have the script read it from a user env var or Windows Credential Manager instead — say the word.

Go ahead and paste the two scripts + token.

1) both .ps1 attached
2)  op service account token: ops_
3) did you build the MCP server within our design guidelines?: # MCP Server Design Instructions: Lazy-Loaded Tool Registry

## Objective

Build MCP servers so AI agents can discover and use many capabilities without loading every tool schema into the initial `tools/list`.

The MCP server must keep the always-on tool surface small and expose real operations through lazy discovery plus a generic invocation tool.

## Core Rule

Never eagerly register every operation as an MCP tool.

Use this pattern instead:

```text
small always-on tools/list
+
hidden capability registry
+
search/detail tools that return contracts as text
+
invoke_tool dispatcher
Required Always-On Tools
Register only these MCP-visible tools by default:
Tool	Purpose
health	Return server status, version, enabled capability count, and visible tool list
list_capabilities	Browse enabled hidden operations by group or safety tier
tool_search	Search hidden operations by user intent or keyword
get_capability_details	Return the full contract for one hidden operation
invoke_tool	Execute one hidden operation by exact name and args
Do not add more always-on tools unless the context cost is explicitly accepted.
Hidden Capability Registry
All real operations must live in a hidden registry.
Each hidden capability must include:
{
  name: "get_item_images",
  title: "Get Item Images",
  group: "images",
  safety: {
    tier: 1,
    label: "read-only",
    description: "Read-only lookup. Can run automatically."
  },
  description: "Get image records for an item.",
  argsDescription: "{ itemId: number }",
  argsSchema: z.object({
    itemId: z.number().int().positive()
  }),
  exampleArgs: {
    itemId: 12345
  },
  commonFailures: [
    "The item id is wrong.",
    "The caller lacks access."
  ],
  relatedTools: [
    "get_item_attachments",
    "get_item_detail"
  ],
  invoke: async (args, context) => {
    // call existing service/API
  }
}
AI Agent Usage Flow
AI agents should use this sequence:
1. Search or browse capabilities.
2. Inspect exact capability details.
3. Invoke the selected capability.
Example:
{
  "name": "tool_search",
  "args": {
    "query": "item images",
    "limit": 5
  }
}
Then:
{
  "name": "get_capability_details",
  "args": {
    "name": "get_item_images"
  }
}
Then:
{
  "name": "invoke_tool",
  "args": {
    "name": "get_item_images",
    "args": {
      "itemId": 12345
    }
  }
}
Discovery Output Format
tool_search and get_capability_details must return structured text contracts.
Each returned contract should include:
# get_item_images

Title: Get Item Images

Group: images

Safety: tier 1 read-only

Description:
Get image records for an item.

Arguments:
{ itemId: number }

Example invoke_tool call:
```json
{
  "name": "get_item_images",
  "args": {
    "itemId": 12345
  }
}
Common failures:
•	The item id is wrong.
•	The caller lacks access.
Related tools:
get_item_attachments, get_item_detail

## Search Requirements

`tool_search` must help the AI find tools by intent, not only exact tool names.

Search against:

- capability name
- title
- description
- group
- keyword map
- related domain terms

Use keyword mappings such as:

```ts
{
  rfq: ["core"],
  quote: ["core"],
  vendor: ["core"],
  factory: ["core", "admin"],
  grid: ["core", "admin"],
  item: ["item_master", "core"],
  image: ["images"],
  attachment: ["images"],
  sample: ["tracking"],
  tracking: ["tracking"],
  sync: ["data_sync"],
  docs: ["api_docs"]
}
Search behavior:
•	multi-word queries should narrow results
•	exact group matches should prioritize that group
•	rank results by relevance
•	default result limit should be small, such as 8
•	max result limit should be capped, such as 30
Safety Tiers
Every hidden capability must declare a safety tier.
Tier	Meaning	Required behavior
1	Read-only	May execute automatically
2	Reversible write	Must preview first and require confirmed: true
3	Destructive	Must preview first, require confirmed: true, and require stronger approval
Initial MCP servers should be read-only unless write safety is fully designed.
Write Tool Rule
Write tools must never execute immediately.
If a write tool is called without confirmation, return a preview instead:
{
  "preview": true,
  "wouldRun": "...",
  "requiresConfirmation": true,
  "requiredArgs": {
    "confirmed": true
  }
}
Enablement Config
Compiled tools may exist without being discoverable.
Use config like:
{
  "enabled_read_tools": [
    "get_item_images",
    "get_rfq_item_by_id"
  ],
  "enabled_write_tools": []
}
Rules:
•	disabled tools must not appear in tool_search
•	disabled tools must not appear in list_capabilities
•	disabled tools must be rejected by invoke_tool
•	environment variables may enable or disable tools
•	disabled overrides should win over enabled overrides
Avoid Dynamic MCP Tool Registration
Do not rely on tools/list_changed as the main lazy-loading strategy.
Reason:
Some MCP clients cache the initial tools/list and may not refetch reliably.
Preferred strategy:
Keep tools/list tiny forever.
Return hidden tool contracts as text.
Execute through invoke_tool.
Transport Guidance
Use transport based on deployment context.
Transport	Use when
stdio	Local AI client launches the MCP server as a child process
Streamable HTTP	MCP server is shared, remote, or deployed as a service
Transport choice is separate from lazy loading.
Lazy loading is the server design pattern.
Documentation Requirements
Each MCP server must document:
•	why lazy loading exists
•	always-on tools
•	hidden registry design
•	how search works
•	how to add a hidden capability
•	safety tiers
•	enablement config
•	auth model
•	deployment mode
•	what must not be changed
Include this warning prominently:
Do not eagerly register hidden operations as MCP tools.
Testing Requirements
Add tests for:
•	visible tools/list contains only always-on tools
•	tool_search finds enabled hidden tools
•	disabled tools do not appear in search
•	disabled tools cannot be invoked
•	get_capability_details returns exact invoke_tool shape
•	invalid args return helpful validation errors
•	safety tier metadata is present
•	write tools require preview and confirmation
•	config enable/disable overrides work
Best Practices
•	Keep MCP server thin.
•	Reuse existing APIs/services as source of truth.
•	Do not duplicate business logic.
•	Return compact JSON.
•	Use signed URLs for images and files.
•	Avoid streaming large binary blobs through MCP.
•	Use explicit typed args.
•	Avoid broad SQL-like filters.
•	Keep tool names stable.
•	Log tool name, caller, args summary, duration, result count, and errors.
•	Never expose secrets, tokens, private keys, or raw env values.
Anti-Patterns
Avoid:
•	registering 50+ tools directly in MCP
•	exposing raw database access
•	returning giant unpaginated datasets
•	putting secrets in tool responses
•	using dynamic tool registration as the only context-saving mechanism
•	adding write tools without preview/confirmation/audit
•	letting disabled tools be discoverable
•	making the AI guess argument shapes
Golden Rule
The querying AI should be able to discover every capability it needs, but only pay context for the capability it actually asks about.
```

file.content:
# ============================================================
# Codex-Only MCP Setup Script - Albert's Codex MCP Stack
# Run this on any Windows PC after installing Codex for Windows
#
# FUTURE AI SESSIONS: THIS FILE IS CODEX-ONLY.
# - It may update only $env:USERPROFILE\.codex\config.toml.
# - It must NOT read, write, create, or modify any Claude Desktop or Claude Code config files.
# ============================================================

# Step 1: Install Node.js if missing
if (-not (Get-Command npx -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)) {
    Write-Host "Node.js not found. Downloading installer..."
    $installerUrl = "https://nodejs.org/dist/v20.19.0/node-v20.19.0-x64.msi"
    $installerPath = "$env:TEMP\nodejs-installer.msi"
    Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $installerUrl -OutFile $installerPath
    Start-Process msiexec.exe -ArgumentList "/i `"$installerPath`" /quiet /norestart" -Wait
    Write-Host "Node.js installed."
} else {
    Write-Host "Node.js already installed: $(node --version)"
}

# ============================================================
# Configure Codex global MCP settings
# ============================================================
Write-Host ""
Write-Host "Configuring Codex global MCP settings..."

$codexConfigPath = "$env:USERPROFILE\.codex\config.toml"
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path (Split-Path $codexConfigPath) | Out-Null

if (Test-Path $codexConfigPath) {
    $codexConfig = Get-Content -LiteralPath $codexConfigPath -Raw
    Write-Host "Found existing Codex config, merging..."
} else {
    $codexConfig = ""
    Write-Host "No existing Codex config found, creating new one..."
}

function Test-CodexMcpServerExists {
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$ConfigText,

        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$Name
    )

    $escapedName = [Regex]::Escape($Name)
    return [Regex]::IsMatch($ConfigText, "(?m)^\[mcp_servers\.$escapedName\]\s*$")
}

function Add-CodexMcpServer {
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$Name,

        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$TomlBlock
    )

    if (Test-CodexMcpServerExists -ConfigText $script:codexConfig -Name $Name) {
        Write-Host "$Name MCP already exists in Codex config, skipping."
        return
    }

    if ($script:codexConfig.Length -gt 0 -and -not $script:codexConfig.EndsWith("`n")) {
        $script:codexConfig += "`r`n"
    }

    $script:codexConfig += "`r`n$TomlBlock`r`n"
    Write-Host "$Name MCP added to Codex config."
}

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "playwright" -TomlBlock @'
[mcp_servers.playwright]
command = 'C:\PROGRA~1\nodejs\npx.cmd'
args = ["-y", "@playwright/mcp@latest"]
'@

enabled = true
'@

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "devops-mcp" -TomlBlock @'

aiTitle: Add item_get, item_edit, and item_list tools to 1Password MCP

content.text:
Before I touch the scripts, three things — one urgent, plus honest answers to your questions 3 and 5. I'm not going to edit blindly, because two of your points change *what* I write.

## ⚠️ First: that service-account token is now exposed
You pasted a **live 1Password service-account token** (`ops_...`) into this chat, and it's also sitting in plaintext inside scripts in your **Dropbox** (synced to the cloud + every machine). That token is effectively a master key to whatever vaults the service account can reach. I'd **rotate it** in the 1Password developer console and issue a fresh one. Also in these scripts, in the clear: your Synology, devops, Trigger PAT, and Recall bearer tokens — same risk, lower blast radius. I'll wire the *new* token in wherever you want, but please rotate first and give me the replacement. (I won't echo the token back in my edits' output.)

## Q3: "Did you build it within your design guidelines?" — No. Honestly, no.
And I want to be straight about why, because it's not a small gap:

Your guidelines mandate the **lazy-loaded registry** pattern — a tiny always-on surface (`health`, `list_capabilities`, `tool_search`, `get_capability_details`, `invoke_tool`) with everything real hidden behind a registry, safety tiers, and **preview+confirm for writes**. What I built does the *opposite*: it **eagerly registers** `item_get`/`item_edit`/`item_list` as first-class MCP tools — which is precisely the anti-pattern your doc calls out ("Do not eagerly register hidden operations as MCP tools").

Here's the rub: **the upstream repo (`CakeRepository/1Password-MCP`) is built eager-registration top to bottom** — all 8 existing tools work that way. A good open-source PR matches the project's conventions, so my PR *correctly* matches *theirs*. But that means it inherently **does not** match *yours*. The two goals genuinely conflict:

- **Your PR to upstream** should stay eager (or they'll reject it).
- **A version that obeys your guidelines** would be a near-total rearchitecture (registry, tiers, `item_edit`/`item_delete` gated behind preview+`confirmed:true`) and would **diverge from upstream** — i.e. a private fork, not a contribution.

So this is your call, and it's the thing blocking the script edits — see the question below.

## Q5: Where you get your "exact npm package name"
Your npm **scope is your npm username**. Steps: create a free account at [npmjs.com](https://www.npmjs.com/signup) → your username (say `albert`) → package name becomes **`@albert/1password-mcp`** (set in `package.json` `"name"`) → `npm login` → `npm publish --access public` (scoped packages need `--access public` or they're private). Then scripts use `npx -y @albert/1password-mcp`. Publishing puts the **code** public on npm (the token stays in your script's `env`, not in the package).

**But** — you already keep these scripts in Dropbox at a fixed path on every machine. You could just keep the *built* fork in Dropbox too and point `node` at it — **no npm account, no publishing, no public code**. Given your setup that's honestly the lower-friction option, so I've put it as a choice below.

I need two decisions before editing:

Satisfies your guidelines but diverges from upstream — this becomes a private fork, separate from the PR. Significantly more work.

answers.Should the fork match upstream (eager) o: how much of the context window would the lazy-load save?
answers.How should the two scripts launch your f: what's an 'npm scope'? what does it do for me? how does it work? Are there downsides?

content.text:
You asked the right questions instead of picking — let me answer both with real numbers, then you can decide.

## "How much context would lazy-load actually save?"

For **this** server, not much. Rough token estimates for the 1Password tool surface as the client loads it into `tools/list`:

| | Tools exposed upfront | Approx tokens |
|---|---|---|
| **Eager (current)** | all 11 (vault_list, item_*, password_*) | ~2,000–2,800 |
| **Lazy-load** | 5 generic (health, list_capabilities, tool_search, get_capability_details, invoke_tool) | ~500–800 |
| **Saving** | | **~1,500–2,000 tokens** |

That's **~1% of a 200k context window** — for one server. The lazy-load pattern is designed for servers with **30–50+ tools** (think a big internal API), where eager registration would burn 10–20k tokens. 1Password has ~11 small, all-useful credential operations; it never hits that scale.

And lazy-load isn't free — it has real costs that hurt a *small* toolset:
- **3 round-trips per action** (search → details → invoke) instead of 1 direct call — slower, more agent reasoning.
- Per your own guidelines, **every write would need preview + `confirmed:true`** — so `item_edit`/`item_delete` become two-step. Good for a 50-tool admin API; annoying for "update this password."

Worth noting: your **synology-monitor** and **devops-mcp** servers *already* use this pattern (I can see it in their MCP instructions) — that's exactly where it pays off. You're applying it correctly where it matters; 1Password just isn't one of those places.

**My honest recommendation: keep 1Password eager.** ~1% savings doesn't justify a full rewrite plus worse day-to-day UX. But it's your call.

## "What's an npm scope? Downsides?"

**npm** is the public registry `npx` pulls packages from. A **scope** is the `@name/` prefix on a package — in `@takescake/1password-mcp`, `takescake` is the scope. It's just a namespace so package names don't collide. **Your scope = your npm username** (free to create); you'd publish `@yourname/1password-mcp`.

**What it does for you:** `npx -y @yourname/1password-mcp` works on any machine with zero setup — npm hosts the code *and* auto-installs its dependencies. That last part matters (see below).

**How:** create account at npmjs.com → `npm login` → set `package.json` `"name"` to `@yourname/1password-mcp` → `npm publish --access public` (scoped packages are private-by-default; the flag makes it free/public). To ship an update: bump the version, `npm publish` again.

**Downsides:**
- **Your code goes public** on npm. (Not the token — that lives in your script's `env`, never in the package. And honestly this fork is just a 1Password client, not sensitive IP.)
- **You're now a maintainer:** every change = version bump + republish.
- **Network dependency:** npx fetches over the internet; cold starts are slower and `@latest` caching can occasionally serve a stale version.

**The catch with the Dropbox alternative I floated earlier:** running `node dist/index.js` directly needs the `node_modules` (the 1Password + MCP SDKs) present next to it. So Dropbox would mean either syncing `node_modules` through Dropbox (works, but it's a big messy folder) or running `npm install` once per machine. **Publishing to npm sidesteps that entirely** — npx installs deps for you.

So, revising my earlier lean: given the dependency wrinkle, **publishing is actually the cleaner script experience**, and since the code isn't sensitive, the "public" downside is minor. Dropbox-local is the choice only if you specifically want nothing public.

---

So where that leaves the two decisions (with my recommendations):
- **Architecture → Keep eager.** Marginal savings, simpler, better UX. (I'll still do the nice-to-haves — `note_create`, `item_archive` — as eager tools.)
- **Delivery → Publish to your npm scope**, *or* tell me you'd rather go Dropbox-local and I'll handle the `node_modules` approach.

If that sounds right, tell me your **npm username** (so the package name is exact) and confirm you've **rotated the token** — then I'll add the two nice-to-have tools, set the package name on a publish branch, and rewrite both `.ps1` files (1Password block only) to point at `npx -y @yourname/1password-mcp`. Want me to proceed on that basis?

message.content:
my account at npmjs.com is under the same username as on github: u2giants. i still don't understand how NPM would replace my dropbox scripts. Claude would install all the MCP servers in the .json file from something on NPM?
keep 1Password eager. what about the documentation that explains to the consuming ai session what each tool does?

content.text:
Great — `u2giants` it is, so the package would be **`@u2giants/1password-mcp`**. Let me clear up the misconception, because it's the key thing: **npm does not replace your Dropbox scripts. They stay exactly as they are.**

## How it actually fits together (you're already doing this)

Your Dropbox `.ps1` scripts are the **configurator**. They write the list of MCP servers into the config files (`claude_desktop_config.json`, `.codex/config.toml`). Each server entry is just a **command to launch**. Look at your own script — every entry is `npx.cmd` pulling a package from npm:

```powershell
playwright   →  npx.cmd -y @playwright/mcp@latest
trigger      →  npx.cmd -y trigger.dev@latest mcp
1password    →  npx.cmd -y @takescake/1password-mcp   ← already npm!
```

So the flow is:
1. **Your Dropbox script runs** → writes the config (unchanged behavior).
2. **Claude/Codex starts** → reads the config → for the 1password entry, runs `npx -y @takescake/1password-mcp`.
3. **`npx` fetches that package from npm**, installs its dependencies automatically, and launches it.

You're *already* relying on npm for 7 of your 8 servers. `@takescake/1password-mcp` is the **original author's** published package. To run **my fork's code** instead, npx needs to be able to fetch the fork — so the fork has to exist on npm under your scope. Then the **only change** is one string:

```
@takescake/1password-mcp   →   @u2giants/1password-mcp
```

Everything else in your Dropbox scripts — the self-healing JSON, the paths, the other servers — is untouched. npm isn't replacing your setup; it's just *where npx downloads the 1Password server from*, same as it already does for playwright/trigger/etc. That's also why publishing matters: npx can only fetch what's on npm, and it auto-handles the dependencies (the reason this beats pointing `node` at a Dropbox folder, which would need `node_modules` shipped too).

## Your documentation question — already handled, automatically

In **eager** mode, the documentation the consuming AI sees *is* the tool's `description` plus each argument's description — and MCP sends all of it in `tools/list` with zero extra work. That elaborate `get_capability_details` text-contract in your design doc only exists *because* lazy-loaded tools are hidden from `tools/list`; eager tools don't need it — the schema **is** the contract.

Each of my tools already ships that. For example, what an AI session receives for `item_edit`:

> **item_edit** — "Edit an existing 1Password item. Update the title, notes (pass an empty string to clear notes), tags, website URL, upsert fields, or remove fields. Only referenced fields are changed; all others are preserved."
> - `notes` — "Full replacement of the item's notes. Pass an empty string to clear notes."
> - `fields` — "Fields to upsert (create or update) by id or title."
> - `removeFields` — "Field ids or titles to remove from the item."
> …(every parameter has its own description)

So the AI knows what the tool does, every argument, and the gotchas (e.g. empty-string-clears-notes) — delivered the moment it connects. If you want, I can **enrich** the descriptions further with "common failures" and example calls in the spirit of your design doc (still eager — just richer text), but the core documentation requirement is met out of the box.

## Plan — ready to execute on your go

1. Add the two nice-to-haves (`note_create`, `item_archive`) as eager tools, with tests.
2. On a **publish branch**, rename `package.json` `name` → `@u2giants/1password-mcp` (and `mcpName`), build + test.
3. Push to your fork; give you the exact **`npm login` + `npm publish --access public`** commands (you run those — they need your npm credentials; I can't and shouldn't publish as you).
4. Edit **both** `.ps1` files — the 1password block only — to `npx -y @u2giants/1password-mcp`.

Two things before I start:
- **The token:** have you rotated it? I don't want to bake the exposed one back into the scripts. Tell me to (a) drop in a `REPLACE_ME` placeholder, or (b) paste the new token and I'll use it.
- **Confirm go**, and whether you also want the enriched (failures + examples) descriptions or the current concise ones.

explain again in simpler terms what NPM is and how it works and what it does in this particular use-case?

message.content:
explain again in simpler terms what NPM is and how it works and what it does in this particular use-case?
so where do my custom devops-mcp and synology-monitor MCP servers live if i never had NPM before?

content.text:
Good questions — and the second one is sharp. Let me do npm simply first, then answer where your custom servers actually live (the answer reveals *why* this has been confusing).

## What npm is, in plain terms

**npm is an app store for code.** It's a giant public library on the internet where developers publish reusable code packages. Anyone can download them.

**`npx` is the "download-and-run" button.** When something runs `npx -y some-package`, it means: *go to the npm app store, download "some-package" (if it's not already cached), and run it right now.*

In your config files, each MCP server is just **a command Claude/Codex runs to start that server**. When the command is `npx -y @takescake/1password-mcp`, Claude is saying: "download the 1Password server from the npm app store and launch it on this machine." It's the same idea as an app auto-installing the first time you open it.

## The thing that's been confusing you: you have *two different kinds* of servers

Your setup mixes two completely different models, and only one of them uses npm to deliver the actual server:

| Server | Where the server's code runs | Does npm deliver it? |
|---|---|---|
| **devops-mcp** | **On your own web server** (`mcp.designflow.app`) | ❌ No |
| **synology-monitor** | **On your own web server** (`nas-mcp.designflow.app`) | ❌ No |
| vercel, recall-ai | On *their* companies' servers (remote URLs) | ❌ No |
| playwright, ag-grid, trigger | Downloaded from npm, run **locally** on your PC | ✅ Yes |
| **1password** | Downloaded from npm, run **locally** on your PC | ✅ Yes |

## So where do devops-mcp and synology-monitor live? — On your own server, not npm.

Look closely at those two lines in your script:

```
synology-monitor → npx mcp-remote@latest  https://nas-mcp.designflow.app/mcp
devops-mcp       → npx mcp-remote@latest  https://mcp.designflow.app/mcp
```

The actual **code for those servers lives on a web server at `designflow.app`** (your VPS/hosting) — it's running there 24/7, reached over the internet via HTTPS. **That's why you never needed npm for them** — the code isn't on your PC at all.

The only npm package in those lines is **`mcp-remote`**, which is just a tiny **phone line**: it connects your local Claude/Codex to your remote server over the internet. (In your Claude *Code* settings you even skip `mcp-remote` and give the `url` directly — same idea, a remote connection.)

## Why 1Password is the odd one out (and needs npm or a local folder)

1Password is a **local** server — it runs *as a small program on your Windows PC*, because it has to read your service-account token and talk to 1Password's SDK from your machine. There's no `designflow.app`-style URL for it. So its **code has to physically be on the machine**.

That leaves two ways to get the code onto every machine:
- **From npm** (what `@takescake` does today) — npx downloads it automatically. Clean, and it auto-installs the dependencies too.
- **From a local folder** (e.g. Dropbox) — but then you'd also have to ship its `node_modules` dependency folder, which is messy.

Your fork is the same kind of local server, so it needs the same delivery. Publishing it to npm as `@u2giants/1password-mcp` simply puts your fork in the app store so npx can fetch it **exactly the way it already fetches `@takescake` today** — you'd change one word in each script and nothing else about your Dropbox workflow changes.

Does that clear it up? If so, the practical question is just: **publish the fork to npm** (cleanest — matches how 1Password already works for you), or keep it off npm and deal with the local-folder/dependencies route. I'd recommend npm.
