toolUseResult.originalFile:
# 1Password MCP Server

[![CI](https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP/actions/workflows/ci.yml)
[![npm](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/@takescake/1password-mcp)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@takescake/1password-mcp)
[![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-Apache--2.0-blue)](LICENSE)

A community-built [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/) server that connects MCP-compatible AI clients (Claude Desktop, VS Code Copilot, OpenAI Codex, Gemini, etc.) to **1Password** vaults via a [Service Account](https://developer.1password.com/docs/service-accounts/).

> **Not an official 1Password product.** This is a community project.

---

## Features

### Tools (11)

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `vault_list` | List all accessible vaults |
| `item_lookup` | Search items by title in a vault |
| `item_list` | List all items in a vault (id, title, category, tags, updatedAt) |
| `item_get` | Retrieve a full item (title, category, tags, notes, fields); conceals secret values unless `reveal` is true |
| `item_edit` | Edit an item's title, notes, tags, URL, and fields (upsert/remove); empty `notes` clears notes |
| `item_delete` | Delete an item from a vault |
| `password_create` | Create a new password/login item |
| `password_read` | Retrieve a password via secret reference (`op://vault/item/field`) or vault/item ID |
| `password_update` | Rotate/update an existing password |
| `password_generate` | Generate a cryptographically secure random password |
| `password_generate_memorable` | Generate a memorable passphrase from ~500 dictionary words |

### Prompts (4)

| Prompt | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `generate-secure-password` | Guided workflow to generate and store a secure password |
| `credential-rotation` | Step-by-step credential rotation: read, generate, update, verify |
| `vault-audit` | Audit vault contents: list items, categorize, flag concerns |
| `secret-reference-helper` | Construct `op://vault/item/field` references interactively |

### Resources (3)

| Resource URI | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `1password://config` | Current server configuration (non-secret) |
| `1password://vaults` | Browsable list of all accessible vaults |
| `1password://vaults/{vaultId}/items` | Browsable list of items in a vault |

---

## Quick Start

### Prerequisites

- **Node.js** >= 18
- A [1Password Service Account token](https://developer.1password.com/docs/service-accounts/)

### Claude Desktop / VS Code / IDEs (JSON)

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "1password": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@takescake/1password-mcp"],
      "env": {
        "OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN": "YOUR_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### macOS Keychain (JSON)

If you do not want to store the service account token directly in your MCP config, macOS users can store it in Keychain and configure the server to read it at startup instead:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "1password": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@takescake/1password-mcp"],
      "env": {
        "OP_KEYCHAIN_SERVICE": "op-service-account-claude-automation",
        "OP_KEYCHAIN_ACCOUNT": "your-macos-username"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Precedence is: CLI arguments (`--service-account-token` / `--token`) > `OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN` > macOS Keychain lookup. `OP_KEYCHAIN_ACCOUNT` is optional if your Keychain service name is already unique enough.

### OpenAI Codex (TOML)

**Option A** (stores the token in config):

```toml
[mcp_servers."1password"]
command = "npx"
args = ["-y", "@takescake/1password-mcp"]

[mcp_servers."1password".env]
OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN = "YOUR_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN"
```

**Option B** *(recommended: does NOT store the token in Codex config)*:

```toml
[mcp_servers."1password"]
command = "npx"
args = ["-y", "@takescake/1password-mcp"]
env_vars = ["OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN"]
```

Then set `OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN` in your shell/session/CI environment.

> **Note:** `codex mcp add ... --env OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN=...` writes the token into Codex config. Use `env_vars` if you want the config to reference only the variable name.

On macOS, you can also omit `OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN` and set `OP_KEYCHAIN_SERVICE` (plus optional `OP_KEYCHAIN_ACCOUNT`) to read the token from Keychain at startup.

### CLI Options

```
--service-account-token <token>   1Password service account token
--log-level <level>               Log level: error, warn, info, debug (default: info)
--integration-name <name>         Custom integration name for 1Password SDK
--integration-version <version>   Custom integration version
```

---

## Security & Privacy

> **Read this before using.**

- **LLM privacy risk** -- Secrets retrieved/created may be sent to your LLM provider and could be retained depending on your provider/account settings.
- **No E2E encryption in MCP** -- Secrets are plaintext inside the MCP workflow and in transit to the model. They are encrypted only once stored in 1Password.
- **Intended use** -- Best for automated/disposable credentials (dev DB creds, bot/service accounts, CI tokens).
- **Avoid high-stakes secrets** -- Do not use for banking, primary personal accounts, or other sensitive credentials. Use dedicated automation vaults.
- **Token security** -- Treat the Service Account Token like a master key. Rotate immediately if exposed.
- **Config files** -- Keep MCP config files out of version control (add to `.gitignore`).
- **Secret references** -- Prefer `op://...` references over copying raw passwords into prompts or files.
- **Least privilege** -- Use dedicated vaults and limited-scope service accounts for automation workflows.

---

## Development

```bash
# Clone and install
git clone https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP.git
cd 1Password-MCP
npm install

# Build
npm run build

# Run tests
npm test

# Type-check
npm run lint

# Watch mode (dev)
npm run dev
```

### Project Structure

```
src/
  index.ts              # Server entrypoint
  types.ts              # Shared type definitions
  logger.ts             # Structured logging (stderr)
  config.ts             # CLI args, env vars, constants
  client.ts             # 1Password SDK client singleton
  utils.ts              # Result helpers, password generation
  tools/                # MCP tool handlers
    index.ts
    vault-list.ts
    item-lookup.ts
    item-delete.ts
    password-create.ts
    password-read.ts
    password-update.ts
    password-generate.ts
    password-generate-memorable.ts
  prompts/              # MCP prompt definitions
    index.ts
  resources/            # MCP resource definitions
    index.ts
```

See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for contribution guidelines.

---

## License

[Apache License 2.0](LICENSE)

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# ============================================================
# Codex-Only MCP Setup Script - Albert's Codex MCP Stack
# Run this on any Windows PC after installing Codex for Windows
#
# FUTURE AI SESSIONS: THIS FILE IS CODEX-ONLY.
# - It may update only $env:USERPROFILE\.codex\config.toml.
# - It must NOT read, write, create, or modify any Claude Desktop or Claude Code config files.
# ============================================================

# Step 1: Install Node.js if missing
if (-not (Get-Command npx -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)) {
    Write-Host "Node.js not found. Downloading installer..."
    $installerUrl = "https://nodejs.org/dist/v20.19.0/node-v20.19.0-x64.msi"
    $installerPath = "$env:TEMP\nodejs-installer.msi"
    Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $installerUrl -OutFile $installerPath
    Start-Process msiexec.exe -ArgumentList "/i `"$installerPath`" /quiet /norestart" -Wait
    Write-Host "Node.js installed."
} else {
    Write-Host "Node.js already installed: $(node --version)"
}

# ============================================================
# Configure Codex global MCP settings
# ============================================================
Write-Host ""
Write-Host "Configuring Codex global MCP settings..."

$codexConfigPath = "$env:USERPROFILE\.codex\config.toml"
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path (Split-Path $codexConfigPath) | Out-Null

if (Test-Path $codexConfigPath) {
    $codexConfig = Get-Content -LiteralPath $codexConfigPath -Raw
    Write-Host "Found existing Codex config, merging..."
} else {
    $codexConfig = ""
    Write-Host "No existing Codex config found, creating new one..."
}

function Test-CodexMcpServerExists {
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$ConfigText,

        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$Name
    )

    $escapedName = [Regex]::Escape($Name)
    return [Regex]::IsMatch($ConfigText, "(?m)^\[mcp_servers\.$escapedName\]\s*$")
}

function Add-CodexMcpServer {
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$Name,

        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$TomlBlock
    )

    if (Test-CodexMcpServerExists -ConfigText $script:codexConfig -Name $Name) {
        Write-Host "$Name MCP already exists in Codex config, skipping."
        return
    }

    if ($script:codexConfig.Length -gt 0 -and -not $script:codexConfig.EndsWith("`n")) {
        $script:codexConfig += "`r`n"
    }

    $script:codexConfig += "`r`n$TomlBlock`r`n"
    Write-Host "$Name MCP added to Codex config."
}

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "playwright" -TomlBlock @'
[mcp_servers.playwright]
command = 'C:\PROGRA~1\nodejs\npx.cmd'
args = ["-y", "@playwright/mcp@latest"]
'@

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "synology-monitor" -TomlBlock @'
[mcp_servers.synology-monitor]
command = 'C:\PROGRA~1\nodejs\npx.cmd'
args = ["-y", "mcp-remote@latest", "https://nas-mcp.designflow.app/mcp", "--transport", "http-first", "--header", "Authorization: Bearer 14cde11e584136b15306c03d160ce9536da4f87f82d74c6d728a6c8cb6dd2122"]

'@

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "devops-mcp" -TomlBlock @'

args = ["-y", "mcp-remote@latest", "https://mcp.designflow.app/mcp", "--transport", "http-first", "--header", "Authorization: Bearer xBY2IHFwVfXnVUZ3rwfs-zW0jdf4BO2oO8iB1TjRs-0"]

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "vercel" -TomlBlock @'
[mcp_servers.vercel]
command = 'C:\PROGRA~1\nodejs\npx.cmd'

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "ag-grid" -TomlBlock @'

[mcp_servers.trigger.env]
TRIGGER_ACCESS_TOKEN = "tr_pat_yep2ozuh7z9vp9iy4rca38ysmbqmogcka4sajy1p"

content.text:
All edits are in. Both scripts now point at `@u2giants/1password-mcp` (2 spots in the Claude script — Desktop + Code; 1 in Codex), and I left the `env`/token lines exactly as they were.

Here's where things stand and the one part only you can do (publishing needs your npm login).

## ✅ Done by me
- **5 new tools** on the fork, all eager, all tested: `item_get`, `item_edit`, `item_list`, `note_create`, `item_archive` (77 tests green, `tsc` strict, no `any`).
- **Branches pushed to your fork:**
  - `feat/item-get-edit-list` → the upstream PR ([#7](https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP/pull/7), keeps `@takescake` name).
  - `publish/u2giants-scope` → same code, renamed to **`@u2giants/1password-mcp`** for npm. Packs cleanly (`bin` + `dist` included).
- **Both Dropbox scripts edited** — package name only.

## 🔑 Your turn: publish to npm (one time)
You must run these — they need your npm credentials (I can't and shouldn't log in as you):

```powershell
cd C:\repos\temp\1Password-MCP
git checkout publish/u2giants-scope
npm login                       # browser opens; sign in as u2giants
npm whoami                      # should print: u2giants
npm publish --access public     # auto-runs clean+build+test, then publishes
```

`--access public` is required (scoped packages are private by default; public is free). First publish auto-creates your `@u2giants` scope. After it succeeds, `@u2giants/1password-mcp` is live and `npx` can fetch it anywhere.

## ▶️ Then on each machine

file.content:
# ============================================================
# Codex-Only MCP Setup Script - Albert's Codex MCP Stack
# Run this on any Windows PC after installing Codex for Windows
#
# FUTURE AI SESSIONS: THIS FILE IS CODEX-ONLY.
# - It may update only $env:USERPROFILE\.codex\config.toml.
# - It must NOT read, write, create, or modify any Claude Desktop or Claude Code config files.
# ============================================================

# Step 1: Install Node.js if missing
if (-not (Get-Command npx -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)) {
    Write-Host "Node.js not found. Downloading installer..."
    $installerUrl = "https://nodejs.org/dist/v20.19.0/node-v20.19.0-x64.msi"
    $installerPath = "$env:TEMP\nodejs-installer.msi"
    Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $installerUrl -OutFile $installerPath
    Start-Process msiexec.exe -ArgumentList "/i `"$installerPath`" /quiet /norestart" -Wait
    Write-Host "Node.js installed."
} else {
    Write-Host "Node.js already installed: $(node --version)"
}

# ============================================================
# Configure Codex global MCP settings
# ============================================================
Write-Host ""
Write-Host "Configuring Codex global MCP settings..."

$codexConfigPath = "$env:USERPROFILE\.codex\config.toml"
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path (Split-Path $codexConfigPath) | Out-Null

if (Test-Path $codexConfigPath) {
    $codexConfig = Get-Content -LiteralPath $codexConfigPath -Raw
    Write-Host "Found existing Codex config, merging..."
} else {
    $codexConfig = ""
    Write-Host "No existing Codex config found, creating new one..."
}

function Test-CodexMcpServerExists {
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$ConfigText,

        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$Name
    )

    $escapedName = [Regex]::Escape($Name)
    return [Regex]::IsMatch($ConfigText, "(?m)^\[mcp_servers\.$escapedName\]\s*$")
}

function Add-CodexMcpServer {
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$Name,

        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$TomlBlock
    )

    if (Test-CodexMcpServerExists -ConfigText $script:codexConfig -Name $Name) {
        Write-Host "$Name MCP already exists in Codex config, skipping."
        return
    }

    if ($script:codexConfig.Length -gt 0 -and -not $script:codexConfig.EndsWith("`n")) {
        $script:codexConfig += "`r`n"
    }

    $script:codexConfig += "`r`n$TomlBlock`r`n"
    Write-Host "$Name MCP added to Codex config."
}

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "playwright" -TomlBlock @'
[mcp_servers.playwright]
command = 'C:\PROGRA~1\nodejs\npx.cmd'
args = ["-y", "@playwright/mcp@latest"]
'@

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "synology-monitor" -TomlBlock @'
[mcp_servers.synology-monitor]
command = 'C:\PROGRA~1\nodejs\npx.cmd'
args = ["-y", "mcp-remote@latest", "https://nas-mcp.designflow.app/mcp", "--transport", "http-first", "--header", "Authorization: Bearer e014bedde42998fdd6967a2e8de612a788288413dc3313bec0cdce286e7416e6"]
enabled = true
'@

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "devops-mcp" -TomlBlock @'
[mcp_servers.devops-mcp]
command = 'C:\PROGRA~1\nodejs\npx.cmd'
args = ["-y", "mcp-remote@latest", "https://mcp.designflow.app/mcp", "--transport", "http-first", "--header", "Authorization: Bearer dafd7ba23fbfa9889879e372f947dfaf25bc4491e1f45198936da3ebef39ee7f"]
'@

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "vercel" -TomlBlock @'
[mcp_servers.vercel]
command = 'C:\PROGRA~1\nodejs\npx.cmd'
args = ["-y", "mcp-remote@latest", "https://mcp.vercel.com"]

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "ag-grid" -TomlBlock @'
[mcp_servers.ag-grid]

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "trigger" -TomlBlock @'

[mcp_servers.trigger.env]
TRIGGER_ACCESS_TOKEN = "tr_pat_yep2ozuh7z9vp9iy4rca38ysmbqmogcka4sajy1p"

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "recall-ai" -TomlBlock @'

'@

Add-CodexMcpServer -Name "1password" -TomlBlock @'

args = ["-y", "@takescake/1password-mcp"]

[mcp_servers."1password".env]
OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN = "ops_"
'@

$codexConfig | Set-Content -LiteralPath $codexConfigPath -Encoding UTF8
Write-Host "Codex config updated."

input.content:
# Publishing this fork to npm (`@u2giants/1password-mcp`)

This fork is published to npm under the **`@u2giants`** scope so the Windows
setup scripts can launch it with `npx -y @u2giants/1password-mcp`, exactly the
way they launch the other npm-based MCP servers.

> This file documents the **fork's** release process. It is intentionally NOT
> part of the upstream PR to `CakeRepository/1Password-MCP`.

## Where to run these

On **any machine that has this repo cloned + Node.js + internet** — simplest is
the **Windows PC in PowerShell** where the repo already lives
(`C:\repos\temp\1Password-MCP`). npm uploads to npmjs.com over the network, so
the machine doesn't matter as long as you're logged in to npm.

## First-time setup (once per machine)

```powershell
npm login          # opens a browser; sign in as the npm user "u2giants"
npm whoami         # must print: u2giants
```

## Release a new version

From the repo root, on the `publish/u2giants-scope` branch:

```powershell
git checkout publish/u2giants-scope

# 1. Bump the version everywhere it appears (keep these three in sync):
#    - package.json      "version"
#    - server.json       "version" AND packages[0].version
#    - src/config.ts     SERVER_VERSION
#    Or use npm to bump package.json automatically:
npm version patch   # 2.4.2 -> 2.4.3  (use "minor" for new features)

# 2. Publish (this auto-runs clean + build + test via prepublishOnly):
npm publish --access public
```

`--access public` is required — scoped packages are private by default, and
public publishing is free.

## After publishing

Nothing else to do. `npx -y @u2giants/1password-mcp` on any machine picks up the
new version automatically (npm serves the latest). Just restart Claude
Desktop / Claude Code / Codex so they relaunch the server.

## Where things live

- **This server's code (the fork):** https://github.com/u2giants/1Password-MCP
  - `publish/u2giants-scope` branch → the version published to npm.
  - `feat/item-get-edit-list` branch → the PR back to the original project.
- **npm package:** https://www.npmjs.com/package/@u2giants/1password-mcp (after first publish)
- **Upstream project:** https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP
- **The Windows setup scripts** (`setup-claude-mcps.ps1`, `setup-codex-mcps.ps1`)
  live in Dropbox, not in a git repo. They reference `@u2giants/1password-mcp`.

content.text:
All three handled. Summary:

### 1) Scripts re-updated ✅
Re-applied `@takescake` → `@u2giants/1password-mcp` to the **current** versions of both files (Claude script lines 185 + 270, Codex line 126). Your rotated tokens were left untouched. (I can see the other session rotated the 1Password, Synology, and devops tokens — good, that closes the earlier exposure.)

### 2) Where do you run `npm login` / `npm publish`?
**On the Windows machine, in PowerShell** — not the Ubuntu server. Specifically the box where the fork is cloned:

```powershell
cd C:\repos\temp\1Password-MCP
git checkout publish/u2giants-scope
npm login                     # browser opens → sign in as u2giants
npm whoami                    # must print: u2giants
npm publish --access public
```

Why Windows and not Ubuntu: `npm` just uploads the package to npmjs.com over the internet — the OS doesn't matter, but the command has to run **where the code is**, and I cloned/built it on this Windows PC. The Ubuntu server doesn't have this repo. (You *could* publish from Ubuntu if you cloned the fork there, but there's no reason to — stick with the Windows box.)

### 3) Will you have to remember the version-bump? No — it's now in the repo.
**Yes, you have a GitHub repo** — it's your fork: **https://github.com/u2giants/1Password-MCP** (created the moment you forked; I've been pushing branches to it). Two things to know about "do we have a repo":
- **The MCP server fork** → yes, on GitHub (above). The branch published to npm is `publish/u2giants-scope`.
- **The Dropbox setup scripts** → no, those are just files in Dropbox, not a git repo.

I added **[PUBLISHING.md](https://github.com/u2giants/1Password-MCP/blob/publish/u2giants-scope/PUBLISHING.md)** to the publish branch. It records exactly: where to run it, `npm login` once, `npm version patch` then `npm publish --access public` to ship updates, and where every branch/artifact lives. So next time (or the next AI session), it's written down — just open that file.

### Where this leaves you
- **Nothing works until you run the publish commands above** — the package `@u2giants/1password-mcp` doesn't exist on npm yet, so the scripts currently point at something not-yet-published. Run them, then re-run your setup scripts and restart the apps.
- After first publish, the npm page will be: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@u2giants/1password-mcp

Want me to also drop a tiny `publish.ps1` helper next to the repo that does the version-bump + publish in one command, so you don't even need to open PUBLISHING.md?

content.text:
**2) The 403 is a 2FA gate, not a bug in the steps.** The build/test passed and the package was fully prepared — npm just refused the final upload because your npm account requires two-factor auth to publish. The fix is to pass your one-time code:

```powershell
npm publish --access public --otp=123456
```

Replace `123456` with the current 6-digit code from your authenticator app (Google Authenticator/1Password/Authy — wherever you set up npm 2FA). That single command will complete the publish; everything else already succeeded.

- **If you don't have an authenticator linked to npm:** npm now *requires* 2FA to publish. Either go to npmjs.com → your avatar → **Account** → enable **Two-Factor Authentication**, then re-run with `--otp`; **or** create a **Granular Access Token** (Access Tokens → Generate → Granular, with read/write to `@u2giants` and "bypass 2FA" enabled) and publish with that. The OTP route is faster for a one-time publish.

Let me also clean up that harmless warning npm printed and document the 2FA step so it's not a surprise next time:

content.text: Clean one-line fix (silences that warning on future publishes). Let me update `PUBLISHING.md` with the 2FA step and commit both:

input.old_string:
# 2. Publish (this auto-runs clean + build + test via prepublishOnly):
npm publish --access public
```

`--access public` is required — scoped packages are private by default, and
public publishing is free.
input.new_string:
# 2. Publish (this auto-runs clean + build + test via prepublishOnly).
#    npm requires two-factor auth to publish, so pass your 6-digit code:
npm publish --access public --otp=123456   # replace 123456 with your authenticator code
```

`--access public` is required — scoped packages are private by default, and
public publishing is free.

### Two-factor authentication (the `--otp` flag)

npm requires 2FA to publish. If you omit `--otp` you'll get
`npm error code E403 ... Two-factor authentication ... is required`. Two ways
to satisfy it:

- **Interactive (one-off):** add `--otp=<6-digit code>` from the authenticator
  app linked to your npm account.
- **Unattended (lets an AI session publish for you):** create a **Granular
  Access Token** at npmjs.com (Access Tokens → Generate → Granular) with
  read/write on the `@u2giants` scope and **"bypass 2FA" enabled**, then store
  it once on the machine:
  `npm config set //registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=<token>`. After that,
  `npm publish --access public` works with no prompt — so a future AI session
  can do the whole release by following this file.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Publishing this fork to npm (`@u2giants/1password-mcp`)

This fork is published to npm under the **`@u2giants`** scope so the Windows
setup scripts can launch it with `npx -y @u2giants/1password-mcp`, exactly the
way they launch the other npm-based MCP servers.

> This file documents the **fork's** release process. It is intentionally NOT
> part of the upstream PR to `CakeRepository/1Password-MCP`.

## Where to run these

On **any machine that has this repo cloned + Node.js + internet** — simplest is
the **Windows PC in PowerShell** where the repo already lives
(`C:\repos\temp\1Password-MCP`). npm uploads to npmjs.com over the network, so
the machine doesn't matter as long as you're logged in to npm.

## First-time setup (once per machine)

```powershell
npm login          # opens a browser; sign in as the npm user "u2giants"

**3) You're right — skip the `publish.ps1`. You understood it correctly.**

A helper script would just be *one more thing you run manually*, which isn't better than an AI session reading PUBLISHING.md and running the commands. So I won't add it.

But here's the real constraint worth being clear about, because it's the crux: **whoever runs `npm publish` needs your npm credentials.** Right now publishing needs your 2FA code, which lives on your phone — an AI session can't produce that, so today *you* have to be the one to run the final command (or paste the OTP).

The way to make "AI handles everything" actually true: do the **granular token with 2FA-bypass** once (it's in PUBLISHING.md now). Store it on the machine with `npm config set //registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=<token>`. After that, `npm publish` needs no prompt — so a future AI session can bump the version and publish end-to-end just by following PUBLISHING.md. No script, no your-involvement. That's the setup that matches what you want; it's a one-time token step, your call whether to do it.

---

### Where you are right now
- ✅ Both `.ps1` files updated + sent to you.
- ✅ Fork code published-ready on branch `publish/u2giants-scope` (build + 77 tests green during your run).
- ⏳ **One command left to actually publish** — run this with a fresh code from your authenticator:
  ```powershell

  ```
- After it succeeds: package goes live at https://www.npmjs.com/package/@u2giants/1password-mcp → re-run your setup scripts → restart Claude Desktop / Claude Code / Codex → smoke-test with "list items in vault `<id>`".

One small note: I committed a `git+https` tweak to `package.json` *after* your failed publish attempt, so when you re-run publish it'll use the latest commit — no version bump needed since 2.4.2 was never actually published (the upload was rejected). If npm ever says "you cannot publish over the previously published version 2.4.2," that just means a prior attempt squeaked through — then bump with `npm version patch` and re-run.

message.content: npmjs.com no longer supports otp 2fa. can we do this with an access token?

content.text:
Yes — access token is now the right way (npm is deprecating OTP-for-publish in favor of tokens). A **Granular Access Token** works without any OTP prompt.

## Step 1 — Create the token (on npmjs.com, ~1 min)
1. Sign in at npmjs.com → click your **avatar** (top-right) → **Access Tokens**.
2. **Generate New Token** → **Granular Access Token**.
3. **Name:** e.g. `1password-mcp-publish`.
4. **Expiration:** your choice (e.g. 90 days).
5. **Permissions → Packages and scopes:** set to **Read and write**.
   - ⚠️ Gotcha: the package doesn't exist yet, so it won't appear in the per-package list. Either select your **`@u2giants` scope** with read/write, or if the scope isn't selectable, choose **"All packages"** read/write just for this first publish (you can tighten it afterward).
6. **Generate** and copy the token (starts with `npm_…`).

> Simpler fallback if granular is fiddly for a first publish: **Generate New Token → Classic → Automation**. Automation tokens bypass 2FA and can publish any package — broader access, but foolproof for the initial publish.

## Step 2 — Publish with it
```powershell
cd C:\repos\temp\1Password-MCP
git checkout publish/u2giants-scope
npm config set //registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=npm_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
npm publish --access public
```
That `npm config set` line overwrites the web-login credential from earlier with your token, so the publish goes through with no OTP. (It writes to your user `~/.npmrc` — same plaintext-secret tradeoff as your other tokens.)

This token is also what enables **future hands-off publishes** — once it's in `~/.npmrc`, a later AI session can bump the version and run `npm publish --access public` with no prompt, exactly the "AI handles everything" flow you wanted. It's now documented in [PUBLISHING.md](https://github.com/u2giants/1Password-MCP/blob/publish/u2giants-scope/PUBLISHING.md).

---

**Want me to run the publish for you?** Since these tool calls execute on your Windows machine, if you paste the token here I'll run the `npm config set` + `npm publish` and confirm it went live. Only caveat: the token would pass through this chat (same exposure as the other secrets you've shared). If you'd rather keep it out of chat, just run the two commands yourself and tell me the result.

message.content: before i do any of this, how do i get that token into 1password so that all ai sessions can access it in the future?

content.text: Great instinct — storing it in 1Password is far better than leaving it in `~/.npmrc`, and you already have the 1Password MCP wired into your AI sessions, so this closes the loop nicely. Let me check what your service account can actually reach so I give you a concrete spot rather than hand-waving.

Your service account can read exactly **one vault: `vibe_coding`** (id `b2dsir4jze3wfygdxixoaasdeq`). That's the key fact — an AI session reaches 1Password *through* this service account, so the npm token **must live in `vibe_coding`** or no future session can read it. Let me check it's not already there:

Perfect setup, and `vibe_coding` is empty of npm items, so we start clean. Here's the complete picture.

## The key constraint
An AI session reaches 1Password **only through your service account**, and that account can see **only the `vibe_coding` vault**. So the npm token must be stored **there** — anywhere else and no future session can read it.

## Where to put it (so it has a stable address)
Store it as a **Password item** in `vibe_coding`:
- **Title:** `npm-publish-token`
- **Token value** → the item's **password** field

That gives it a permanent "secret reference": **`op://vibe_coding/npm-publish-token/password`** — which is how any future session fetches it.

## Two ways to create the item
- **A) In the 1Password app (token never touches this chat):** New item → Password → vault `vibe_coding` → title `npm-publish-token` → paste the token into the password field.
- **B) Let me create it via the MCP:** paste the token here and I'll run `password_create` into `vibe_coding` for you (one caveat: requires your service account to have *write* access to the vault, and the token passes through chat).

## How future AI sessions will use it (the loop you're building)
1. Session reads the token via the 1Password MCP: `password_read` on `op://vibe_coding/npm-publish-token/password`.
2. Sets it: `npm config set //registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=<value>`.
3. `npm publish --access public`.

No `~/.npmrc` secret sitting around permanently, no OTP, centralized + rotatable.

## One honest tradeoff to be clear about
If you want AI sessions to publish **automatically**, they must be able to **read** the token — which means the AI *sees* it (in the MCP read result and when setting npm config) during that session. That's unavoidable for any "AI handles everything" flow. What 1Password storage buys you over `~/.npmrc` is real regardless: the secret isn't parked in plaintext on every machine, it's access-controlled to the service account, and it only materializes transiently when a publish actually runs. If you'd rather *no* AI ever see it, then publishing stays a manual step — you can't have both.

Let me record this pattern in the repo so it's repeatable:

# Publishing this fork to npm (`@u2giants/1password-mcp`)

This fork is published to npm under the **`@u2giants`** scope so the Windows
setup scripts can launch it with `npx -y @u2giants/1password-mcp`, exactly the
way they launch the other npm-based MCP servers.

> This file documents the **fork's** release process. It is intentionally NOT
> part of the upstream PR to `CakeRepository/1Password-MCP`.

## Where to run these

On **any machine that has this repo cloned + Node.js + internet** — simplest is
the **Windows PC in PowerShell** where the repo already lives
(`C:\repos\temp\1Password-MCP`). npm uploads to npmjs.com over the network, so
the machine doesn't matter as long as you're logged in to npm.

## First-time setup (once per machine)

```powershell
npm login          # opens a browser; sign in as the npm user "u2giants"
npm whoami         # must print: u2giants
```

## Release a new version

From the repo root, on the `publish/u2giants-scope` branch:

```powershell
git checkout publish/u2giants-scope

# 1. Bump the version everywhere it appears (keep these three in sync):
#    - package.json      "version"
#    - server.json       "version" AND packages[0].version
#    - src/config.ts     SERVER_VERSION
#    Or use npm to bump package.json automatically:
npm version patch   # 2.4.2 -> 2.4.3  (use "minor" for new features)

# 2. Publish (this auto-runs clean + build + test via prepublishOnly).
#    npm requires two-factor auth to publish, so pass your 6-digit code:
npm publish --access public --otp=123456   # replace 123456 with your authenticator code
```

`--access public` is required — scoped packages are private by default, and
public publishing is free.

### Two-factor authentication (the `--otp` flag)

npm requires 2FA to publish. If you omit `--otp` you'll get
`npm error code E403 ... Two-factor authentication ... is required`. Two ways
to satisfy it:

- **Interactive (one-off):** add `--otp=<6-digit code>` from the authenticator
  app linked to your npm account.
- **Unattended (lets an AI session publish for you):** create a **Granular

  can do the whole release by following this file.

Quick note on that message: **Trusted Publishing** (OIDC) is npm's recommended path *for CI/CD* — it lets a GitHub Actions workflow publish with **no stored token at all**. It's genuinely the most secure option, but it only works from a CI runner (e.g., GitHub Actions), not from a local machine or an AI session. Since your flow is local/AI-driven publishing, a stored token is still the right tool here. (If you ever want, I can set up a GitHub Actions release workflow using Trusted Publishing and retire the token entirely — separate project.)

content.text:
Honest result: **still blocked** — same 403. The token authenticated fine (`npm whoami` → u2giants), but npm rejected the publish with:

> *"...granular access token with bypass 2fa enabled is required to publish packages."*

That tells us exactly what's wrong: **the granular token you created does not have the "bypass 2FA" capability enabled.** That's the very option that showed you the "security risks with bypassing 2fa" warning — npm nudges you toward Trusted Publishing there, so it's easy to leave it off. Without that toggle on, the token can authenticate but cannot publish while your account enforces 2FA.

Two ways to fix it — pick one:

### Option A (simplest, recommended): Classic **Automation** token
Automation tokens bypass 2FA by design — no toggle to hunt for.
- npmjs.com → avatar → **Access Tokens** → **Generate New Token** → **Classic Token** → select **Automation** → Generate.

### Option B: Re-make the **Granular** token with bypass ON
- Same steps as before, but this time **enable the "bypass 2FA" option** and accept that warning. Read+write on `@u2giants` (or All packages for the first publish).

Either way, **paste the new token** and I'll:
1. Update the `npm-publish-token` item in 1Password (so it stays the single source of truth),
2. Re-auth and run `npm publish --access public`,
3. Confirm it's live.

> Side note now that you've seen this friction twice: npm is clearly steering hard toward **Trusted Publishing** (token-free, OIDC from GitHub Actions). If this keeps being painful, the durable fix is a tiny GitHub Actions release workflow on your fork — you'd publish by pushing a tag, no token to store or bypass at all. Say the word and I'll set that up as a follow-up. For right now, an Automation token is the fastest way to get 2.4.2 live.

The non-working token is still in 1Password — I'll overwrite it with the new one so nothing stale lingers.

Anything else you want to verify or wire up, or are you all set?

message.content:
Upstream PR #7: i don't know what this means. just do whatever is best
Trusted Publishing: if we go this route does that mean i have another piece of software i have to install or something i have to do on every computer i vibe-code on?

content.text:
Both good questions — plain answers:

## Upstream PR #7 — what it is, and what I'll do
In plain terms: when you "forked" the 1Password MCP server, you made your own copy of someone else's open-source project. PR #7 is an **offer to give your 5 new tools back to that original project**, so other people who use the original also get your improvements. Think of it as "here's a useful addition, want it?" sent to the original author.

- It's **completely optional** and **needs nothing from you** — the original author decides whether to accept it. Your own published `@u2giants` version doesn't depend on it at all.
- It costs you nothing to leave it sitting there, and if accepted, you get credit as a contributor.

**What's best: I'll leave it open.** It's complete, tested, and well-described (it already lists all 5 tools). There's genuinely nothing for you to do or maintain — if the author never looks at it, no harm; if they merge it, bonus. So: handled, ignore it.

## Trusted Publishing — no, nothing on your computers
**You would NOT install anything, and nothing changes on the computers you vibe-code on.** That's the key point. Here's the split:

- **The consuming side** (your Windows machines running Claude/Codex) — **totally unchanged.** They still just `npx -y @u2giants/1password-mcp`. Trusted Publishing has zero effect here.
- **The publishing side** (how a new version gets *uploaded* to npm) — this is the only thing that would change, and it moves **off your machines entirely** onto GitHub's servers.

How it'd work: a small config file lives **in the GitHub repo** (not on any of your PCs). To release a new version, you'd push a git tag, and GitHub's servers build + publish to npm automatically using a cryptographic handshake — **no npm token stored anywhere**. So it actually *removes* the token from 1Password rather than adding anything.

**My honest recommendation: don't bother right now.** You already have a working, hands-off publish flow (token safely in 1Password, AI can publish for you). Trusted Publishing is a "nice to have" that trades a bit of one-time GitHub setup for never managing a token again. It's worth doing only if token management starts to annoy you. If that day comes, say the word and I'll set it up — it's a one-time thing on the repo, never on your machines.

So: PR left open (nothing for you to do), and Trusted Publishing parked unless you want it. You're fully set up — the published fork with all new capabilities is working in your sessions. Anything else?

content.text:
Found it. Your config has **two** `[mcp_servers."1password"]` blocks — the old `@takescake` one (lines 110–115, with your old rotated-out token) and the new `@u2giants` one (lines 118–123). TOML forbids duplicate keys, hence the crash. 

Root cause: the script's "already exists?" check looks for `[mcp_servers.1password]` but the block is written quoted as `[mcp_servers."1password"]`, so it never matched and appended a second copy. I'll remove the stale block (which also deletes the old exposed token), then fix the script so it can't recur.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
﻿# ============================================================
# Codex-Only MCP Setup Script - Albert's Codex MCP Stack
# Run this on any Windows PC after installing Codex for Windows
#
# FUTURE AI SESSIONS: THIS FILE IS CODEX-ONLY.
# - It may update only $env:USERPROFILE\.codex\config.toml.
# - It must NOT read, write, create, or modify any Claude Desktop or Claude Code config files.
# ============================================================

# Step 1: Install Node.js if missing
if (-not (Get-Command npx -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)) {
    Write-Host "Node.js not found. Downloading installer..."
    $installerUrl = "https://nodejs.org/dist/v20.19.0/node-v20.19.0-x64.msi"
    $installerPath = "$env:TEMP\nodejs-installer.msi"
    Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $installerUrl -OutFile $installerPath
    Start-Process msiexec.exe -ArgumentList "/i `"$installerPath`" /quiet /norestart" -Wait
    Write-Host "Node.js installed."
} else {
    Write-Host "Node.js already installed: $(node --version)"
}

# ============================================================
# Configure Codex global MCP settings
# ============================================================
Write-Host ""
Write-Host "Configuring Codex global MCP settings..."

$codexConfigPath = "$env:USERPROFILE\.codex\config.toml"
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path (Split-Path $codexConfigPath) | Out-Null

if (Test-Path $codexConfigPath) {
    $codexConfig = Get-Content -LiteralPath $codexConfigPath -Raw
    Write-Host "Found existing Codex config, merging..."
} else {
    $codexConfig = ""
    Write-Host "No existing Codex config found, creating new one..."
}

function Test-CodexMcpServerExists {
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$ConfigText,

        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$Name
    )

    $escapedName = [Regex]::Escape($Name)
    return [Regex]::IsMatch($ConfigText, "(?m)^\[mcp_servers\.$escapedName\]\s*$")
}

function Add-CodexMcpServer {
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$Name,

        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
        [string]$TomlBlock
    )

    if (Test-CodexMcpServerExists -ConfigText $script:codexConfig -Name $Name) {
        Write-Host "$Name MCP already exists in Codex config, skipping."
        return
    }

    if ($script:codexConfig.Length -gt 0 -and -not $script:codexConfig.EndsWith("`n")) {
        $script:codexConfig += "`r`n"
    }

    $script:codexConfig += "`r`n$TomlBlock`r`n"
    Write-Host "$Name MCP added to Codex config."
}

content.text:
The file's already saved at your Dropbox path (the tool to re-attach it isn't available this turn, but the fix is in place). Here's everything:

## 1) Codex — fixed ✅
Removed the duplicate `1password` block (and the old token), repaired the script's guard so re-running never duplicates again. **Reopen your Codex thread** and it'll load.

## 2) Offer the 5 tools back to the original project — already done ✅
That offer *is* PR #7, and it's **open** right now: **https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP/pull/7** — title "feat: add item_get, item_edit, item_list, note_create, and item_archive tools," targeting the original project's `master`. So your 5 tools are formally offered back; the original author can accept whenever. Nothing more needed from you.

## 3) Trusted Publishing — detailed prompt for a fresh AI session
You're right to do it: your token expires **~2026-09-20** (90 days from creation), and Trusted Publishing removes that recurring task entirely. Copy everything in the box below into a new session (run it from the repo, ideally on this same machine where `C:\repos\temp\1Password-MCP` lives):

````text
TASK: Set up npm Trusted Publishing (OIDC, token-free) for my forked MCP server so I never have to manage or rotate an npm publish token again.

BACKGROUND / FACTS (verify before acting):
- GitHub repo (my fork): https://github.com/u2giants/1Password-MCP  (gh CLI is authed as user "u2giants")
- npm package: @u2giants/1password-mcp  (PUBLIC, scope @u2giants, npm username u2giants). Currently at version 2.4.2, already published.
- Local clone: C:\repos\temp\1Password-MCP  (Windows; use PowerShell or the Bash tool).
- RELEASE BRANCH = publish/u2giants-scope. IMPORTANT: this branch's package.json is named "@u2giants/1password-mcp". The "master" branch is intentionally named "@takescake/1password-mcp" because it backs an upstream PR — do NOT publish from master. All Trusted Publishing work (workflow file, releases, tags) must be based on publish/u2giants-scope.
- Build: `npm run build` (tsc). Test: `npm test` (vitest). CI matrix Node 18/20/22. package.json has a prepublishOnly that runs clean+build+test.
- Today there is a granular npm token stored in 1Password at op://vibe_coding/npm-publish-token/password (expires ~2026-09-20). The GOAL is to stop depending on it.
- A PUBLISHING.md exists on publish/u2giants-scope documenting the current token-based flow — update it to describe the new token-free flow.

WHAT TO BUILD:
1) A GitHub Actions workflow on the publish/u2giants-scope branch (e.g. .github/workflows/release.yml) that publishes to npm via Trusted Publishing:
   - Triggers on pushing a git tag matching "v*" (e.g. v2.4.3), OR on publishing a GitHub Release — pick tag-push and document it.
   - Job runs on ubuntu-latest, checks out the publish/u2giants-scope ref, sets up Node 20 with registry-url https://registry.npmjs.org, runs `npm ci`, `npm run build`, `npm test`, then `npm publish --access public`.
   - MUST include `permissions: { id-token: write, contents: read }` (OIDC is what makes it token-free).
   - MUST use a recent npm CLI that supports Trusted Publishing (npm >= 11.5.0); add a step `npm install -g npm@latest` if the runner's npm is older. Do NOT add NODE_AUTH_TOKEN / NPM_TOKEN — Trusted Publishing uses OIDC, no secret.
   - Provenance is automatic under Trusted Publishing; don't hand-manage it.
2) A version-bump helper or clear docs: bumping a release means updating the version in ALL of: package.json, server.json (top-level "version" AND packages[0].version), and src/config.ts (SERVER_VERSION) — keep them in sync — then commit, tag v<version>, and push the tag.
3) Update PUBLISHING.md: new flow = "bump version in the 3 files, push a v* tag, GitHub Actions publishes automatically; no token." Note the consuming side (npx -y @u2giants/1password-mcp in the Windows setup scripts) is UNCHANGED.

MANUAL STEPS I (the human) MUST DO — give me exact click-by-click instructions, because an AI cannot configure these:
- On npmjs.com: open the @u2giants/1password-mcp package → Settings → "Publishing access" / "Trusted Publisher" → add a GitHub Actions trusted publisher with: organization/user = u2giants, repository = 1Password-MCP, workflow filename = release.yml, and (if you used one) the environment name. Tell me precisely what to enter.
- Confirm whether I need to set npm's "Publishing access" to allow Trusted Publishing (and whether to also require 2FA-or-automation — clarify the right setting so the OIDC publish isn't blocked).

VERIFY END-TO-END:
- Do a real release: bump to 2.4.3, push tag v2.4.3, watch the Actions run succeed, and confirm `npm view @u2giants/1password-mcp version` shows 2.4.3.

AFTER IT WORKS (cleanup):
- Revoke the old granular npm token on npmjs.com (Access Tokens).
- Update the 1Password item op://vibe_coding/npm-publish-token (vault "vibe_coding") — either delete it or rewrite its note to say "DEPRECATED — publishing now uses GitHub Actions Trusted Publishing; no token needed."
- Remove the token-auth instructions from PUBLISHING.md.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Keep changes on publish/u2giants-scope; do not modify master.
- Don't put any secret in the repo or the workflow.
- Report the final Actions run URL and the published version.
````

That's all three handled. The only things left in *your* hands: reopen Codex (works now), and—whenever you feel like it—run that Trusted Publishing prompt in a fresh session. Want me to also drop that prompt into a file in the repo (e.g. `docs/trusted-publishing-task.md`) so it's not buried in this chat?

1	# 1Password MCP Server
2	
3	[![CI](https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP/actions/workflows/ci.yml)
4	[![npm](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/@takescake/1password-mcp)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@takescake/1password-mcp)
5	[![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-Apache--2.0-blue)](LICENSE)
6	
7	A community-built [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/) server that connects MCP-compatible AI clients (Claude Desktop, VS Code Copilot, OpenAI Codex, Gemini, etc.) to **1Password** vaults via a [Service Account](https://developer.1password.com/docs/service-accounts/).
8	
9	> **Not an official 1Password product.** This is a community project.
10	
11	---
12	
13	## Features
14	
15	### Tools (8)
16	
17	| Tool | Description |
18	|------|-------------|
19	| `vault_list` | List all accessible vaults |
20	| `item_lookup` | Search items by title in a vault |
21	| `item_delete` | Delete an item from a vault |
22	| `password_create` | Create a new password/login item |
23	| `password_read` | Retrieve a password via secret reference (`op://vault/item/field`) or vault/item ID |
24	| `password_update` | Rotate/update an existing password |
25	| `password_generate` | Generate a cryptographically secure random password |
26	| `password_generate_memorable` | Generate a memorable passphrase from ~500 dictionary words |
27	
28	### Prompts (4)
29	
30	| Prompt | Description |
31	|--------|-------------|
32	| `generate-secure-password` | Guided workflow to generate and store a secure password |
33	| `credential-rotation` | Step-by-step credential rotation: read, generate, update, verify |
34	| `vault-audit` | Audit vault contents: list items, categorize, flag concerns |
35	| `secret-reference-helper` | Construct `op://vault/item/field` references interactively |
36	
37	### Resources (3)
38	
39	| Resource URI | Description |
40	|-------------|-------------|
41	| `1password://config` | Current server configuration (non-secret) |
42	| `1password://vaults` | Browsable list of all accessible vaults |
43	| `1password://vaults/{vaultId}/items` | Browsable list of items in a vault |
44	
45	---
46	
47	## Quick Start
48	
49	### Prerequisites
50	
51	- **Node.js** >= 18
52	- A [1Password Service Account token](https://developer.1password.com/docs/service-accounts/)
53	
54	### Claude Desktop / VS Code / IDEs (JSON)
55	
56	```json
57	{
58	  "mcpServers": {
59	    "1password": {
60	      "command": "npx",
61	      "args": ["-y", "@takescake/1password-mcp"],
62	      "env": {
63	        "OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN": "YOUR_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN"
64	      }
65	    }
66	  }
67	}
68	```
69	
70	### macOS Keychain (JSON)
71	
72	If you do not want to store the service account token directly in your MCP config, macOS users can store it in Keychain and configure the server to read it at startup instead:
73	
74	```json
75	{
76	  "mcpServers": {
77	    "1password": {
78	      "command": "npx",
79	      "args": ["-y", "@takescake/1password-mcp"],
80	      "env": {
81	        "OP_KEYCHAIN_SERVICE": "op-service-account-claude-automation",
82	        "OP_KEYCHAIN_ACCOUNT": "your-macos-username"
83	      }
84	    }
85	  }
86	}
87	```
88	
89	Precedence is: CLI arguments (`--service-account-token` / `--token`) > `OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN` > macOS Keychain lookup. `OP_KEYCHAIN_ACCOUNT` is optional if your Keychain service name is already unique enough.
90	
91	### OpenAI Codex (TOML)
92	
93	**Option A** (stores the token in config):
94	
95	```toml
96	[mcp_servers."1password"]
97	command = "npx"
98	args = ["-y", "@takescake/1password-mcp"]
99	
100	[mcp_servers."1password".env]
101	OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN = "YOUR_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN"
102	```
103	
104	**Option B** *(recommended: does NOT store the token in Codex config)*:
105	
106	```toml
107	[mcp_servers."1password"]
108	command = "npx"
109	args = ["-y", "@takescake/1password-mcp"]
110	env_vars = ["OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN"]
111	```
112	
113	Then set `OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN` in your shell/session/CI environment.
114	
115	> **Note:** `codex mcp add ... --env OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN=...` writes the token into Codex config. Use `env_vars` if you want the config to reference only the variable name.
116	
117	On macOS, you can also omit `OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN` and set `OP_KEYCHAIN_SERVICE` (plus optional `OP_KEYCHAIN_ACCOUNT`) to read the token from Keychain at startup.
118	
119	### CLI Options
120	
121	```
122	--service-account-token <token>   1Password service account token
123	--log-level <level>               Log level: error, warn, info, debug (default: info)
124	--integration-name <name>         Custom integration name for 1Password SDK
125	--integration-version <version>   Custom integration version
126	```
127	
128	---
129	
130	## Security & Privacy
131	
132	> **Read this before using.**
133	
134	- **LLM privacy risk** -- Secrets retrieved/created may be sent to your LLM provider and could be retained depending on your provider/account settings.
135	- **No E2E encryption in MCP** -- Secrets are plaintext inside the MCP workflow and in transit to the model. They are encrypted only once stored in 1Password.
136	- **Intended use** -- Best for automated/disposable credentials (dev DB creds, bot/service accounts, CI tokens).
137	- **Avoid high-stakes secrets** -- Do not use for banking, primary personal accounts, or other sensitive credentials. Use dedicated automation vaults.
138	- **Token security** -- Treat the Service Account Token like a master key. Rotate immediately if exposed.
139	- **Config files** -- Keep MCP config files out of version control (add to `.gitignore`).
140	- **Secret references** -- Prefer `op://...` references over copying raw passwords into prompts or files.
141	- **Least privilege** -- Use dedicated vaults and limited-scope service accounts for automation workflows.
142	
143	---
144

146	
147	```bash
148	# Clone and install
149	git clone https://github.com/CakeRepository/1Password-MCP.git
150	cd 1Password-MCP
151	npm install
152	
153	# Build
154	npm run build
155	
156	# Run tests
157	npm test
158	
159	# Type-check
160	npm run lint
161	
162	# Watch mode (dev)
163	npm run dev
164	```

aiTitle: Add item_get, item_edit, and item_list tools to 1Password MCP

content.text:
Honestly — you don't need to, at least not for me. I only reached for Python to double-check the TOML, and I already confirmed the Codex fix a different way (exactly one `1password` block, valid structure). **Node.js is already on this box, and it covers essentially everything I'd want** — I don't have any pending task that needs Python. So please don't install it just on my account.

That said, if you'd like Python there anyway (it's broadly useful), on Windows 11 the cleanest way is **winget**, which is built in:

```powershell
winget install Python.Python.3.12
```

Then **close and reopen the terminal** (so PATH refreshes) and verify:

```powershell
python --version
```

Two notes specific to what you hit:
- The error you saw earlier ("Python was not found… install from the Microsoft Store") is Windows' **App Execution Alias** stub, not a real Python. A proper install via winget shadows it. If `python` still opens the Store after installing, turn off the stub: **Settings → Apps → Advanced app settings → App execution aliases** → toggle **off** `python.exe` and `python3.exe`.
- Alternative if you prefer a GUI: download from [python.org](https://www.python.org/downloads/windows/) and **check "Add python.exe to PATH"** in the installer.

But to be clear: nothing we're doing is blocked on Python. If you install it, great, I'll happily use it; if not, no impact. Want to move on to anything else?
